title: Banyan Retail - Integration Starter Session
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-10-06
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: 45 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Rina Patel (Redwood SE), Diego Marquez (CSM) — Maya Chen (Banyan), Tom Rivers (SRE), Sofia Alvarez (PM), Ethan Cole (Security)

Summary:
Quick alignment on onboarding timeline, network and security constraints (VPC/KMS/SSO), verification of go-live success criteria, and scheduling an infra deep-dive workshop. Agreed to send runbook, security packet, and sample traffic data.

Topics:
- Current infra and data flow
- Hosted vs Dedicated deployment tradeoffs
- SSO/SAML and audit log / KMS requirements
- Pilot scope and success metrics
- Next-phase rollout and support model

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for making time, um yeah this is Jordan from Redwood, happy to kick off.
[00:12] Maya Chen: Hi Jordan, thanks — quick note, we've got folks on the west coast joining so I'll try to keep things paced.
[00:18] Tom Rivers: hey all, Tom here from infra.
[00:22] Rina Patel: Great — goal for today's session is alignment: we want to capture Banyan's constraints and then propose an initial topology for pilot.
[00:28] Sofia Alvarez: Yes, timeline: product wants a pilot in mid-November, 3 week pilot, followed by staged rollout if ok.
[00:35] Diego Marquez: Awesome, congrats on moving fast. what does mid-November look like re: procurement?
[00:41] Maya Chen: Procurement is in flight, we have a conditional budget; legal needs SOC 2 cert docs and a note on data residency.
[00:49] Jordan Blake: Understood — we'll share SOC 2 attestation and our security pack. Also, quick question on data residency — do you need specific region locking or just auditability?
[00:58] Ethan Cole: Region-locking for PII workflows, yes. And we use AWS KMS for key management today.
[01:06] Rina Patel: Noted — mapping that to options: hosted Redwood allows region pinning for enterprise tiers; Dedicated gives full VPC isolation and customer KMS, private key integration.
[01:15] Tom Rivers: If we do Dedicated, what's the expected ops footprint? We can't have big ops overhead.
[01:21] Rina Patel: Dedicated is mostly managed — Redwood handles runtime and patching, you manage network peering and KMS. We'll propose an autoscale policy so you don't overprovision GPU pools.
[01:30] Sofia Alvarez: Do you have example autoscale policies or an estimation model?
[01:34] Rina Patel: Yes, we have a cost-performance model sheet — I'll share it. It maps sample qps + prompt size to target instance counts and burst capacity.
[01:42] Maya Chen: Could you also show what the failover looks like? Like if our Dedicated capacity spikes, is there an automatic fallback?
[01:48] Jordan Blake: We support smart routing — policy-based fallbacks to hosted compatible model variants; can be configured to fail closed or degrade gracefully to lower-tier models.
[01:56] Ethan Cole: We need that in writing for our risk assessment. Also want to know audit log retention limits.
[02:02] Diego Marquez: We'll include retention options; by default enterprise retention is 90 days, with add-on for 1 year.
[02:08] Tom Rivers: On networking, do you prefer private peering or customer-managed VPN? Our infra prefers AWS PrivateLink where possible.
[02:15] Rina Patel: PrivateLink is supported in Dedicated and Private deployments; for Hosted we do egress restrictions and private endpoints in some regions. We'll list tradeoffs in the runbook.
[02:25] Sofia Alvarez: For pilot success metrics — product wants 99% availability and p95 < 250ms for inference on short prompts. Can Redwood hit that?
[02:33] Rina Patel: On short sequences and with proper batching disabled for latency SLOs, our runtime can meet p95 ~80-150ms on our verified models in hosted; dedicated with pinned capacity will be more consistent.
[02:42] Maya Chen: Any gotchas with long conversations and KV caching? We have chat contexts frequently 3-7k tokens.
[02:49] Rina Patel: Longer contexts benefit from KV cache and sequence-aware kernel selection; with larger contexts latency increases and cost per token rises — we'll draft quantization options and cache config.
[02:58] Tom Rivers: And for deployments that handle customer data, how's data-at-rest handled? KMS + encryption?
[03:04] Jordan Blake: Yes, AES-256 with customer-managed KMS in Dedicated/Private; hosted supports envelope encryption with Redwood-managed keys unless you bring your own.
[03:12] Ethan Cole: Is HSM supported for key storage?
[03:15] Rina Patel: HSM integrations are available for Private and Dedicated via partner KMS offerings — we'll include integration notes.
[03:23] Sofia Alvarez: Ok, from product side the pilot scope: two internal apps that use embeddings and chat — low qps but high consistency. We'll provide sample prompts.
[03:33] Diego Marquez: Great — can you share the expected concurrency and token budgets per call?
[03:38] Tom Rivers: we'll send a spreadsheet with p50/p95 p99 traffic and payload sizes.
[03:43] Jordan Blake: Perfect. For the workshop Rina will walk through proposed topology and we'll walk through SSO flow and network set up.
[03:50] Maya Chen: Important: our security team will want a walkthrough of audit logs and RBAC earlier, maybe a separate deep-dive with Ethan.
[03:58] Ethan Cole: yes, please — and include an explanation of who can see token-level data, whether transcripts are stored, and retention controls.
[04:06] Rina Patel: We'll prepare a security annex: RBAC model, token redaction options, transcript storage toggles, and audit events schema.
[04:14] Sofia Alvarez: Any recommended QA / eval plan for the pilot to measure quality regressions?
[04:19] Diego Marquez: We suggest a prompt-set driven eval; Redwood Optimize has a regression alert tied to a test prompt suite — we'll set that up against your baseline.
[04:27] Tom Rivers: What's the typical lead-time to spin up dedicated capacity?
[04:30] Rina Patel: Generally 2-3 weeks after contract + network peering. Hosted pilot can be immediate.
[04:36] Maya Chen: Procurement timeline might be tight — what's the minimum commitment for Dedicated?
[04:40] Jordan Blake: We have monthly reserved tiers, minimal viable commitment for pilot is usually a month for a small pool — we'll outline pricing tiers.
[04:46] Sofia Alvarez: Are there any constraints for regulated PII use?
[04:49] Diego Marquez: For regulated PII, Private deployment or Dedicated with customer KMS is recommended; we also offer eDiscovery and longer audit retention as add-ons.
[04:58] Ethan Cole: Please include sample SOC 2 report and ISO docs in the pack.
[05:02] Jordan Blake: Will do. Also sharing a link to the onboarding runbook and architecture templates — sent as "link: /s/onboarding-runbook" in the follow up.
[05:11] Maya Chen: Perfect.
[05:14] Rina Patel: Next immediate step: schedule the infra workshop — propose Wed 2027-10-12 or Thu 2027-10-13, 2 hours, Rina + Tom + Ethan + Diego.
[05:24] Tom Rivers: Wed works for us.
[05:26] Sofia Alvarez: Also will share product acceptance criteria doc.
[05:29] Jordan Blake: Great. We'll also provide a minimal test harness so you can run a small load test against hosted endpoints if you want to sanity-check latency before provisioning Dedicated.
[05:38] Maya Chen: That'd be helpful.
[05:40] Diego Marquez: Any blockers we should surface?
[05:42] Ethan Cole: Ensure legal has the SLA paragraph for fallback and data access.
[05:46] Jordan Blake: I'll loop in legal to send a redlined SLA.
[05:49] Rina Patel: Action items recap in chat and I'll attach the architecture options.
[05:54] Maya Chen: quick question: if we choose hosted for the pilot, can we request region-specific endpoints just for tests?
[06:01] Rina Patel: Yes, we can provision a hosted region endpoint for enterprise trials — depends on availability in region. We'll confirm.
[06:09] Sofia Alvarez: ok that's everything from product.
[06:11] Jordan Blake: Perfect — thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the runbook, security pack, pricing, and schedule the workshop.
[06:18] Maya Chen: Thanks all, appreciate it.

Closing recap (auto-generated):
- Runbook and security docs to be shared by 2027-10-08
- Infra workshop scheduled for 2027-10-12 (tentative)
- Banyan to provide traffic profiles and acceptance criteria by 2027-10-13
- Redwood to supply pilot pricing and SLA language

References / Links mentioned:
- onboarding runbook: link: /s/onboarding-runbook
- cost model sheet: link: /s/cost-models
- security packet (SOC2/ISO): link: /s/security-packet

Notes on transcription quality: medium — some multi-speaker crosstalk and a few product names mis-heard (e.g., K V cache transcribed as K V cash).

title: Adaptive lookahead + batching cost playbook — Optimize walkthrough with Sequoia Retail
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-10-06
Start: 14:30 UTC
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Asha (Redwood Solutions), Marta, Luis, Sarah, Ben (Sequoia)
Link: https://redwood.app/optimize-demo/seq-2027-10-06

Summary:
Quick Optimize walkthrough focused on adaptive lookahead (prefix / KV cache) + batching recommendations tied to a cost model. Customer provided throughput target (~25 req/s) and 80ms P95 latency target. We discussed sessionization, TTL policy, and tradeoffs between small batches + low latency vs larger batches for unit cost.

Topics covered:
- Current stack and constraints
- Sessionization vs global cache patterns
- Lookahead (prefix) caching heuristics and TTLs
- Batch sizing, concurrency, and autoscale knobs
- Cost-sensitivity spreadsheet walk-through
- Next steps and pilot scope

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros, we'll run through an Optimize playbook that marries lookahead/prefix caching with batching so you can see dollar impact. I'm Jordan, AE for Redwood Inference.
[00:22] Marta: Hi, Marta here. We're excited — our team is trying to reduce per-token cost for chat search features while keeping latency tight.
[00:30] Luis: Luis, platform. We run on GCP, k8s cluster fronting a mix of open models. Throughput target is around 20–30 req/s, peaky.
[00:40] Priya: Great, thanks. Quick perf goals recap? P95 target?
[00:44] Ben: We want P95 <= 80ms and acceptable tail for interactive UI. Latency for embeddings less important.
[00:52] Priya: Good. We'll demo two linked configs: adaptive lookahead (aka prefix / KV caching) plus dynamic batching. We'll show cost modeled at token-level in the workbook.

[02:10] Priya: (shares screen) Here's the Console Optimize tab — you'll see workload heatmap on top, sessionization candidate list in the middle. This is seeded from logs you sent, I smoothed some sessions and grouped by user-id.
[02:35] Marta: Looks like many sessions are repeat prompts with small prefixes, yeah that's our commerce chat pattern.
[03:02] Priya: Exactly — good candidate for prefix caching. Idea: cache first N tokens per session signature so KV hits skip early layers and save compute. Tradeoff is cache TTL and cold-miss amplification.
[03:28] Jordan: Also mention concurrency — caching increases effective batch size because many requests become faster, they can be coalesced into larger batches at the runtime. That lowers per-token cost but can increase headroom requirements.
[03:50] Luis: Headroom as in GPU utilization spikes? We have burst capacity but don't want sustained overcommit.
[03:58] Priya: Right, we model sustained throughput and burst separately. For Sequoia's profile I toggled autoscale burst to 1.5x and reserved capacity to match baseline 25 req/s. See the cost tab.

[05:10] Priya: Walking the cost workbook — columns: scenario name, hit rate, avg tokens, batch size, concurrency, per-token cost, infra cost, net $/1000 requests. I populated three scenarios: baseline (no cache), static prefix cache (TTL 30s), adaptive lookahead (TTL 5m with early evict heuristics).
[05:45] Ben: Can you explain adaptive lookahead heuristics?
[05:49] Priya: Sure — heuristics look at session re-use probability per signature and prefetch probability. If user has a high short-term repeat probability, we keep the prefix in KV with longer TTL; otherwise we evict quickly. There's a small misclassification cost which we model as a 5–12% extra token spend in worst cases.
[06:20] Marta: What about cold-start blowup — when a cached prefix goes stale and then many users request the same cold prefix?
[06:27] Priya: We include amplification factor in the workbook. The safe pattern is staged rollout: start with small fraction of sessions pinned to lookahead, run offline replay for 48h to measure amplification, then widen.

[07:00] Jordan: Let's jump to concrete numbers for Sequoia traces. Baseline: per-1k requests = $4.60. Static TTL 30s with 38% hit rate = $3.20. Adaptive lookahead modeled to hit 62% => $2.10. These are illustrative — your real numbers depend on exact token distribution and your batch-concurrency setup.
[07:40] Luis: Those are big savings, but can you show latency impact?
[07:44] Priya: Yes — look at the latency waterfall. Cached hits are ~12–18ms P95 because they bypass decode-heavy layers (quick KV lookup + small extra kernel). Misses are unchanged. End-to-end P95 for adaptive config is 72ms in sim, within your 80ms goal.
[08:10] Sarah: Are these numbers assuming quantized model variants? We use int8 in prod for cost reasons.
[08:16] Priya: Good point — the workbook has two columns: FP16 baseline and int8 quantized path. Quantization reduces per-token cost by roughly 20–30% in our bench, but can slightly increase latency variance. For your profile we modeled int8 with 18% lower cost; combined with lookahead you hit the $2.10 scenario.

[09:05] Jordan: Real-world gotchas — session signature quality matters. You must ensure consistent session keys (user_id + conversation_id or hashed cookie). If sessionization is noisy you'll see lower effective hit rates.
[09:30] Ben: We're hashing user id and conversation id — should be fine. We do have many anonymous browsers without id.
[09:37] Priya: For anonymous flows, fallback to a short TTL (10–30s) and pair with user-behavior heuristics (IP, recency) — avoids heavy overcache.

[10:20] Priya: Now the demo of Console toggle: we enable adaptive lookahead, set base TTL 300s, early-evict threshold at 0.12 reuse prob, and batch target of 32 tokens per request (we set runtime batch target 64 tokens/batch to encourage packing).
[10:45] Jordan: Small note — runtime continuous batching will pack small requests across 30–40ms windows. If you must hit 50ms strict SLOs, reduce the pack window or set batching disabled for that route.
[11:02] Luis: So can we route high-priority UI calls to lower batching config and low-priority background tasks to high-batch config?
[11:08] Priya: Exactly — Redwood routing supports per-route SLO policies. We'll show an example routing rule in the console after the call.

[12:00] (screen pauses, a short technical exchange)
[13:05] Priya: We also looked at KV sizing. Important: TTL * write rate determines memory size. I put a rough calc — if you have 1M active sessions and TTL 5m average with 1.5KB prefix each, that's ~7.5GB of KV. But write amplification and hot keys need planning.
[13:42] Marta: We don't have 1M active sessions — more like 120k daily active, concurrency peaking at 2k.
[13:50] Priya: Ok then your KV footprint drops substantially. We'll include a sizing tab in the workbook.

[15:00] Ben: Anything we need for security/compliance? We have strict PII rules.
[15:08] Jordan: Good call — Redwood's private and dedicated deployments support KMS/HSM integrations, audit logs, and you can opt to disable KV content persistence (store only hashed prefixes) in Private deploy. We'll attach the security brief.
[15:25] Marta: Can you run the benchmark on our exact model and traces? We want end-to-end numbers including network, egress.
[15:31] Priya: Yes — next step is a 24–48h benchmark run using your anonymized traces. We'll run it in your dedicated sandbox or Redwood hosted sandbox, depending on your comfort with data residency.

[16:10] Jordan: Let's surface a couple of lower-probability risks — misrouted sessions causing cache poisoning (rare if you use proper session keys) and model drift causing different token distributions — both are reasons to keep telemetry and regression alerts.
[16:35] Priya: The Console integrates quality monitors — you can pin prompt sets that should be stable and get alerted when cost-saving changes degrade outputs.

[18:00] (discussion about pricing tiers and reserved capacity)
[18:45] Jordan: If you commit to reserved capacity for peak lanes, we can recommend a commit level that hits your cost targets. The workbook shows reserved vs on-demand infra mix for each scenario.
[19:10] Luis: Any limitations with hybrid private deployment? We need VPC peering and customer-managed KMS.
[19:18] Jordan: Both supported. Private deployment adds 5–10% infra ops overhead in our model due to network egress and encryption CPU — the workbook flags that as an adjustment.

[21:00] Marta: Can you walk slower through the rollout plan? We want a canary and an automated rollback.
[21:12] Priya: Sure — plan: 1) offline replay for 48h to tune TTL/heuristics, 2) 5% canary with live traffic for 24h, monitor amplification and quality, 3) 25% run for 48h, then full rollout. Each step has automatic rollback triggers for hit-rate drop or latency spikes. We can provision a canary route in the Console.

[23:30] (brief bandwidth about cost-sheets — Jordan shares a link)
[23:40] Jordan: I've uploaded the cost-sensitivity workbook here: https://redwood.app/sheets/seq-costs-2027-10-06 (view-only). It includes a tab with your sample traces stats.

[25:15] Ben: A question on metrics — how do you compute hit-rate? Is it by session signature or token-level?
[25:21] Priya: Hit-rate here is session-prefix hit-rate (per-request). We show token-level saved too but it's derived. Sometimes a request has partial prefix hit — we measure effective token-savings.

[27:00] Luis: We want to run a quick experiment: start with TTL 60s and batch target 48 for background jobs. Can Redwood toggle these dynamically?
[27:06] Priya: Yes, Console supports per-route config and dynamic feature flags. You can change TTL and batch target without redeploy.

[29:00] (short off-topic about competitor pricing)
[30:05] Marta: You mentioned amplification risk — can you quantify?
[30:12] Priya: In worst case cold-start storm we modeled ~1.6x token spend for a short window. The workbook's "amplification sensitivity" tab shows how changing canary size and pre-warming reduces that to ~1.1x.

[32:00] Jordan: We'll attach a simple pre-warm script that replays top-N prefixes into KV during deploy to avoid cold storms. It's a standard practice.

[33:00] (customer asks about observability)
[33:05] Asha: Console has per-route token cost, KV hit/miss metrics, batch packing distribution, and trace-level correlation to requests so you can see which prompts cause misses. We'll demo that in follow up.

[35:00] Sarah: Anything else on SLAs? We need 99.9% uptime and audit trails.
[35:10] Jordan: Dedicated and Private offerings come with SLA and extended retention. We'll attach the SLA PDF.

[36:15] Priya: Quick demo of a simulated run — I flipped on adaptive lookahead for 10% of traffic. The console shows slot heatmap: hit rate rose from 0% to 46% in canary, cost down 32% for that cohort, P95 stable at 68ms.

[38:00] Ben: Nice. Can Redwood produce a recommended TTL per route?
[38:10] Priya: Yes, Optimize produces suggested TTLs based on replayed traces and a cost-utility function. It ranks suggestions with expected ROI.

[39:30] Marta: Two follow ups: 1) send workbook, 2) run benchmark with our tracers, 3) show Console playbook for canary.

[40:00] Jordan: We'll do that. Any final questions?
[40:08] Luis: About auditability of KV contents — if we hash prefixes can you still compute hit-rates?
[40:14] Priya: Yes, you can store a salted hash; hit-rate computation works if the same salt is applied consistently. You lose human-readable debug but retain metrics.

[41:00] (wrap up)
[41:10] Jordan: Recap of next steps: I'll send the workbook + security brief, Priya will provision the benchmark and run it once we get traces, Marta will share session logs. We agreed on a 24–48h benchmark then a staged rollout.
[41:35] Marta: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[41:40] Jordan: Thanks — we'll follow up with calendar invites for the benchmark kick-off.

End of transcript.

Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Asha (Redwood Solutions), Marta, Luis, Sarah, Ben (Sequoia)
Link: https://redwood.app/optimize-demo/seq-2027-10-06

Summary:
Quick Optimize walkthrough focused on adaptive lookahead (prefix / KV cache) + batching recommendations tied to a cost model. Customer provided throughput target (~25 req/s) and 80ms P95 latency target. We discussed sessionization, TTL policy, and tradeoffs between small batches + low latency vs larger batches for unit cost.

- Sessionization vs global cache patterns
- Lookahead (prefix) caching heuristics and TTLs
- Batch sizing, concurrency, and autoscale knobs
- Cost-sensitivity spreadsheet walk-through
- Next steps and pilot scope

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros, we'll run through an Optimize playbook that marries lookahead/prefix caching with batching so you can see dollar impact. I'm Jordan, AE for Redwood Inference.
[00:22] Marta: Hi, Marta here. We're excited — our team is trying to reduce per-token cost for chat search features while keeping latency tight.
[00:30] Luis: Luis, platform. We run on GCP, k8s cluster fronting a mix of open models. Throughput target is around 20–30 req/s, peaky.
[00:40] Priya: Great, thanks. Quick perf goals recap? P95 target?
[00:44] Ben: We want P95 <= 80ms and acceptable tail for interactive UI. Latency for embeddings less important.
[00:52] Priya: Good. We'll demo two linked configs: adaptive lookahead (aka prefix / KV caching) plus dynamic batching. We'll show cost modeled at token-level in the workbook.

[02:10] Priya: (shares screen) Here's the Console Optimize tab — you'll see workload heatmap on top, sessionization candidate list in the middle. This is seeded from logs you sent, I smoothed some sessions and grouped by user-id.
[02:35] Marta: Looks like many sessions are repeat prompts with small prefixes, yeah that's our commerce chat pattern.
[03:02] Priya: Exactly — good candidate for prefix caching. Idea: cache first N tokens per session signature so KV hits skip early layers and save compute. Tradeoff is cache TTL and cold-miss amplification.
[03:28] Jordan: Also mention concurrency — caching increases effective batch size because many requests become faster, they can be coalesced into larger batches at the runtime. That lowers per-token cost but can increase headroom requirements.
[03:50] Luis: Headroom as in GPU utilization spikes? We have burst capacity but don't want sustained overcommit.
[03:58] Priya: Right, we model sustained throughput and burst separately. For Sequoia's profile I toggled autoscale burst to 1.5x and reserved capacity to match baseline 25 req/s. See the cost tab.

[05:10] Priya: Walking the cost workbook — columns: scenario name, hit rate, avg tokens, batch size, concurrency, per-token cost, infra cost, net $/1000 requests. I populated three scenarios: baseline (no cache), static prefix cache (TTL 30s), adaptive lookahead (TTL 5m with early evict heuristics).
[05:45] Ben: Can you explain adaptive lookahead heuristics?
[05:49] Priya: Sure — heuristics look at session re-use probability per signature and prefetch probability. If user has a high short-term repeat probability, we keep the prefix in KV with longer TTL; otherwise we evict quickly. There's a small misclassification cost which we model as a 5–12% extra token spend in worst cases.
[06:20] Marta: What about cold-start blowup — when a cached prefix goes stale and then many users request the same cold prefix?
[06:27] Priya: We include amplification factor in the workbook. The safe pattern is staged rollout: start with small fraction of sessions pinned to lookahead, run offline replay for 48h to measure amplification, then widen.

[07:00] Jordan: Let's jump to concrete numbers for Sequoia traces. Baseline: per-1k requests = $4.60. Static TTL 30s with 38% hit rate = $3.20. Adaptive lookahead modeled to hit 62% => $2.10. These are illustrative — your real numbers depend on exact token distribution and your batch-concurrency setup.
[07:40] Luis: Those are big savings, but can you show latency impact?
[07:44] Priya: Yes — look at the latency waterfall. Cached hits are ~12–18ms P95 because they bypass decode-heavy layers (quick KV lookup + small extra kernel). Misses are unchanged. End-to-end P95 for adaptive config is 72ms in sim, within your 80ms goal.
[08:10] Sarah: Are these numbers assuming quantized model variants? We use int8 in prod for cost reasons.
[08:16] Priya: Good point — the workbook has two columns: FP16 baseline and int8 quantized path. Quantization reduces per-token cost by roughly 20–30% in our bench, but can slightly increase latency variance. For your profile we modeled int8 with 18% lower cost; combined with lookahead you hit the $2.10 scenario.

[09:05] Jordan: Real-world gotchas — session signature quality matters. You must ensure consistent session keys (user_id + conversation_id or hashed cookie). If sessionization is noisy you'll see lower effective hit rates.
[09:30] Ben: We're hashing user id and conversation id — should be fine. We do have many anonymous browsers without id.
[09:37] Priya: For anonymous flows, fallback to a short TTL (10–30s) and pair with user-behavior heuristics (IP, recency) — avoids heavy overcache.

[10:20] Priya: Now the demo of Console toggle: we enable adaptive lookahead, set base TTL 300s, early-evict threshold at 0.12 reuse prob, and batch target of 32 tokens per request (we set runtime batch target 64 tokens/batch to encourage packing).
[10:45] Jordan: Small note — runtime continuous batching will pack small requests across 30–40ms windows. If you must hit 50ms strict SLOs, reduce the pack window or set batching disabled for that route.
[11:02] Luis: So can we route high-priority UI calls to lower batching config and low-priority background tasks to high-batch config?
[11:08] Priya: Exactly — Redwood routing supports per-route SLO policies. We'll show an example routing rule in the console after the call.

[12:00] (screen pauses, a short technical exchange)
[13:05] Priya: We also looked at KV sizing. Important: TTL * write rate determines memory size. I put a rough calc — if you have 1M active sessions and TTL 5m average with 1.5KB prefix each, that's ~7.5GB of KV. But write amplification and hot keys need planning.
[13:42] Marta: We don't have 1M active sessions — more like 120k daily active, concurrency peaking at 2k.
[13:50] Priya: Ok then your KV footprint drops substantially. We'll include a sizing tab in the workbook.

[15:00] Ben: Anything we need for security/compliance? We have strict PII rules.
[15:08] Jordan: Good call — Redwood's private and dedicated deployments support KMS/HSM integrations, audit logs, and you can opt to disable KV content persistence (store only hashed prefixes) in Private deploy. We'll attach the security brief.
[15:25] Marta: Can you run the benchmark on our exact model and traces? We want end-to-end numbers including network, egress.
[15:31] Priya: Yes — next step is a 24–48h benchmark run using your anonymized traces. We'll run it in your dedicated sandbox or Redwood hosted sandbox, depending on your comfort with data residency.

[16:10] Jordan: Let's surface a couple of lower-probability risks — misrouted sessions causing cache poisoning (rare if you use proper session keys) and model drift causing different token distributions — both are reasons to keep telemetry and regression alerts.
[16:35] Priya: The Console integrates quality monitors — you can pin prompt sets that should be stable and get alerted when cost-saving changes degrade outputs.

[18:00] (discussion about pricing tiers and reserved capacity)
[18:45] Jordan: If you commit to reserved capacity for peak lanes, we can recommend a commit level that hits your cost targets. The workbook shows reserved vs on-demand infra mix for each scenario.
[19:10] Luis: Any limitations with hybrid private deployment? We need VPC peering and customer-managed KMS.
[19:18] Jordan: Both supported. Private deployment adds 5–10% infra ops overhead in our model due to network egress and encryption CPU — the workbook flags that as an adjustment.

[21:00] Marta: Can you walk slower through the rollout plan? We want a canary and an automated rollback.
[21:12] Priya: Sure — plan: 1) offline replay for 48h to tune TTL/heuristics, 2) 5% canary with live traffic for 24h, monitor amplification and quality, 3) 25% run for 48h, then full rollout. Each step has automatic rollback triggers for hit-rate drop or latency spikes. We can provision a canary route in the Console.

[23:30] (brief bandwidth about cost-sheets — Jordan shares a link)
[23:40] Jordan: I've uploaded the cost-sensitivity workbook here: https://redwood.app/sheets/seq-costs-2027-10-06 (view-only). It includes a tab with your sample traces stats.

[25:15] Ben: A question on metrics — how do you compute hit-rate? Is it by session signature or token-level?
[25:21] Priya: Hit-rate here is session-prefix hit-rate (per-request). We show token-level saved too but it's derived. Sometimes a request has partial prefix hit — we measure effective token-savings.

[27:00] Luis: We want to run a quick experiment: start with TTL 60s and batch target 48 for background jobs. Can Redwood toggle these dynamically?
[27:06] Priya: Yes, Console supports per-route config and dynamic feature flags. You can change TTL and batch target without redeploy.

[29:00] (short off-topic about competitor pricing)
[30:05] Marta: You mentioned amplification risk — can you quantify?
[30:12] Priya: In worst case cold-start storm we modeled ~1.6x token spend for a short window. The workbook's "amplification sensitivity" tab shows how changing canary size and pre-warming reduces that to ~1.1x.

[32:00] Jordan: We'll attach a simple pre-warm script that replays top-N prefixes into KV during deploy to avoid cold storms. It's a standard practice.

[33:00] (customer asks about observability)
[33:05] Asha: Console has per-route token cost, KV hit/miss metrics, batch packing distribution, and trace-level correlation to requests so you can see which prompts cause misses. We'll demo that in follow up.

[35:00] Sarah: Anything else on SLAs? We need 99.9% uptime and audit trails.
[35:10] Jordan: Dedicated and Private offerings come with SLA and extended retention. We'll attach the SLA PDF.

[36:15] Priya: Quick demo of a simulated run — I flipped on adaptive lookahead for 10% of traffic. The console shows slot heatmap: hit rate rose from 0% to 46% in canary, cost down 32% for that cohort, P95 stable at 68ms.

[38:00] Ben: Nice. Can Redwood produce a recommended TTL per route?
[38:10] Priya: Yes, Optimize produces suggested TTLs based on replayed traces and a cost-utility function. It ranks suggestions with expected ROI.

[39:30] Marta: Two follow ups: 1) send workbook, 2) run benchmark with our tracers, 3) show Console playbook for canary.

[40:00] Jordan: We'll do that. Any final questions?
[40:08] Luis: About auditability of KV contents — if we hash prefixes can you still compute hit-rates?
[40:14] Priya: Yes, you can store a salted hash; hit-rate computation works if the same salt is applied consistently. You lose human-readable debug but retain metrics.

[41:00] (wrap up)
[41:10] Jordan: Recap of next steps: I'll send the workbook + security brief, Priya will provision the benchmark and run it once we get traces, Marta will share session logs. We agreed on a 24–48h benchmark then a staged rollout.
[41:35] Marta: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[41:40] Jordan: Thanks — we'll follow up with calendar invites for the benchmark kick-off.

End of transcript.

title: KMS residency, RTO/RPO & escalation runbook readout with Horizon Telecom
preview:
Header:
Date: 2027-06-09
Time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~75 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Samir Kaur (Redwood SE), Miguel Santos (Horizon - Head of Security), Evelyn Chu (Horizon - Cloud Architect), Ravi Patel (Horizon - SRE Lead), Leah Morgan (Horizon - Compliance)

Summary:
This was a security and resilience-oriented readout focused on key management residency, encryption/HSM options, RTO/RPO targets and the customer escalation/runbook flow. Horizon is validating that Redwood's private deployment options meet their KMS residency and audit requirements, and walked through a proposed BCDR runbook and SLA appendix.

Shared artifacts (referenced):
- KMS design sketch (Redwood internal link): https://redwood.app/ht-kms-deck (internal)
- Horizon current runbook excerpt (uploaded to shared folder)
- SLA appendix sample spreadsheet: https://redwood.app/ht-sla-examples

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan Blake: hey everyone thanks for joining, um we'll go, we'll do intros quick and then jump into the KMS design and the runbook readout, sound good?
[00:07] Miguel Santos: yep that's great, Miguel here. thanks Jordan.
[00:09] Evelyn Chu: Evelyn, hi.
[00:11] Ravi Patel: Ravi on SRE.
[00:13] Leah Morgan: Leah, compliance.
[00:15] Priya Nair: Priya Nair, SE on the redwood side. Samir is also with me.
[00:20] Samir Kaur: hey all.

[01:02] Jordan Blake: quick agenda, we have: 1) KMS residency and HSM options — on-prem keys versus cloud KMS, 2) RTO/RPO expectations and failover patterns, 3) escalation chain and communications playbook, then Q&A and next steps.

[02:10] Miguel Santos: quick context from our side, we're in regulated telco, so keys must be either in our control or in a customer-dedicated HSM in the target region. We also require full audit trail retention for seven years and must be able to produce exports for audits.
[02:35] Leah Morgan: and SOC 2 + ISO 27001 audits require clear RBAC, SSO integration, and tamper-evident logs.

[03:05] Priya Nair: understood — Redwood Privy (sic) supports both customer-managed keys via KMS integrations and on-prem HSM proxies for private deployments. we can integrate with cloud KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS) or with your HSM over an on-prem connector. the connector keeps keys on-prem and we do not persist plaintext keys.
[03:22] Evelyn Chu: can you explain the HSM connector flow again? we want to see where TLS terminates and whether the key operations hit our HSM or a Redwood-managed endpoint.

[03:40] Priya Nair: yep. so, at a high level — during private deployment the runtime calls the HSM connector inside your VPC. TLS terminates at the connector which then forwards encrypted tokenized requests to the HSM. we only see key references, not raw keys. note: sometimes the auto-transcript will call it \"Redwood Dedicate\" but it's Redwood Dedicated / Redwood Private depending on deployment.

[05:10] Ravi Patel: what's the lat for key ops? if we do synchronous decryption during token generation, we can't add 400ms per call.
[05:22] Samir Kaur: good question. typical KMS sign/decrypt latency is between 5 and 40ms per op with a colocated connector; worst case 200ms if cross-region. for generation we recommend prefix/KV cache to avoid a KMS call per token. there's a tradeoff — caching vs compliance — if you need every token operation audited and validated, you'll see more KMS traffic.

[07:00] Evelyn Chu: audit logs — what do you capture? we need to know fields, retention, and ability to export in CSV/JSON for auditors.
[07:15] Priya Nair: we capture key usage events, requestor identity (mapped from SAML SSO assertion), timestamp, request trace ID, model version, and the action (encrypt/decrypt/unwrap). retention for hosted is default 90 days but for Dedicated/Private we can extend retention and ship off the logs to your SIEM or to an S3 in-region with lifecycle rules.
[07:47] Miguel Santos: can we get immutable logs — BSI-style — that are tamper-evident?
[07:52] Priya Nair: yes, we can stream to an append-only store you control; we also support log signing via customer keys so you can demonstrate immutability at audit time.

[09:30] Jordan Blake: moving to BCDR: what's your target RTO/RPO for critical inference endpoints?
[09:38] Ravi Patel: for billing-critical and customer-facing paths we want RTO <= 5 minutes ideally, RPO <= 1 minute. for lower-tier internal endpoints 30 minutes / 5 minutes.
[10:05] Priya Nair: noted. Our Dedicated offering can target sub-5 minute failover with pre-warmed standby nodes and cross-region replication of state (KV cache primaries) — though there's a cost tradeoff. we usually recommend a warm-standby approach for sub-5 minute RTOs.
[10:28] Evelyn Chu: what's the mechanism for failing over model variants and session state — do you replicate KV caches?
[10:36] Samir Kaur: we replicate checkpointed KV snapshots to the standby region, with continuous append logs for active sessions. on failover we replay the append logs and resume most sessions within the RPO window, some in-flight context can be lost depending on traffic. we document expected loss rates in the SLA appendix.

[12:00] Miguel Santos: in terms of SLA, we need measurable SLOs, credits, and an escalation ladder that routes to named on-call and exec contacts.
[12:12] Jordan Blake: absolutely — our SLA appendix has tiered credits and explicit escalation points: Tier 1 ops (on-call), Tier 2 engineering lead, Tier 3 service reliability director, and exec escalation to VP/SE when SLA breaches persist. we can map named Horizon contacts to each tier.

[14:10] Leah Morgan: for audit and legal, we also need a documented post-incident review cadence and access to all audit artifacts. how soon do you provide a root cause report?
[14:22] Priya Nair: initial incident summary within 1 business day, full RCA within 7–10 business days for major incidents. we can provide raw traces, sanitized logs, and a timeline export. note that some traces cross multiple systems and may require redaction for multi-tenant logs.

[16:00] Ravi Patel: we want to run an actual tabletop and a live failover test. what does Redwood need from us to run a failover with our KMS setup?
[16:12] Priya Nair: ideal: 1) a test VPC or permission to use a staging VPC with HSM endpoints, 2) test keys or key aliases that are permitted, 3) a window for controlled traffic or a traffic mirror. we'll provide a failover runbook draft and a checklist.

[18:30] Evelyn Chu: how do you handle SSO and RBAC for ops access to keys and runbooks? we require SSO that's federated and role-based access per environment.
[18:42] Samir Kaur: Redwood supports SAML/SCIM and OIDC federation. we map SAML attributes to RBAC roles; we log admin console actions and we can require MFA for privileged actions. for Dedicated we can integrate with your IdP directly inside the VPC.

[21:00] Miguel Santos: about data residency — we have three regions that must keep keys and logs locally: EU-West, APAC-South, and US-East. does Redwood replicate logs cross-region by default?
[21:15] Priya Nair: default replication for dedupe and HA may cross regions unless configured otherwise. on Private deployments we can restrict replication to the same region, or replicate only metadata and keep full logs in-region. we'll need to review your policy doc to ensure controls align.

[23:40] Leah Morgan: small nit — can you sign a SOC2 addendum that lists where audit logs are stored and retention guarantees?
[23:50] Jordan Blake: yes, we have a SOC2 exhibit and can add a schedule for logs and retention for Horizon's dedicated environment. I'll include that in the SLA appendix.

[26:10] Priya Nair: language around escalation — do you want a BCP that auto-escalates at X minutes or is it manual? we can configure auto-escalation policies in the console to contact teams and trigger runbooks.
[26:25] Ravi Patel: auto-escalate for infra P0 after 2 minutes, page Tier 1; if not acknowledged in 10, escalate to Tier 2; after 30 escalate to exec. that model works for us.

[28:00] Samir Kaur: noted — we'll draft a sample escalation schedule matching that timing and include contact placeholders.

[31:20] Miguel Santos: we also want to ensure that our legal team can access a redacted incident timeline for regulators within 48 hours. is that feasible?
[31:33] Jordan Blake: yes, we can deliver a redacted timeline within 48 hours for most incidents; for very complex cross-tenant incidents it might require more time but we provide an interim summary.

title: Kestrel Labs - Private deployment & network onramp discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-04-29 16:00 PT
Duration: 56 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Amir (CTO), Sofia (Networking), Derrick (Security)

Summary: quick discovery focused on private/VPC onboarding, peering constraints, egress/regional gating and SOC2/KMS questions. Customer pretty strict on no public egress and wants regional isolation for EU workloads.

00:00 Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Jordan Blake from Redwood, account exec.
00:07 Amir: Hey Jordan, thanks, Amir here. We, uh, Kestrel Labs — quick context, we're building inference for onboard robotics and some regulated telemetry in EU.
00:15 Jordan: Great, and we have Priya from our solutions team to get into the nitty gritty.
00:18 Priya: Hi all. I'll drive the technical Qs. Sofia, could you give us a bit on your VPC layout and constraints?
00:27 Sofia: Sure. We run a HUB-spoke on AWS, three regions: us-west-2, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1. Our hub is in us-west-2, all traffic transits via transit-gateway. We do not allow internet egress from spokes except via controlled NAT in the hub. We want Redwood to sit in eu-west-1 for EU workloads and us-west-2 for NA.
00:45 Priya: Okay, so you'd prefer regional model instances? Dedicated or private?
00:49 Amir: Prefer private deployment in our VPC if possible. Data residency is a hard constraint — EU data must never leave eu-west-1.
00:55 Jordan: Makes Sense. Two common options: Redwood Private (customer VPC onramp) with private endpoints and peering, or Dedicated with reserved capacity but still behind our control plane. You said private, so we'll focus there.
01:05 Derrick: Quick security note: SOC 2 Type II is table stakes. We also need KMS integration and an HSM path for key wrapping. Can you support customer-managed keys?
01:13 Priya: Yes — our Private offering supports KMS integration. For on-prem HSM we need a brief design review but KMS CMKs in the customer's account are supported for encryption-at-rest.
01:23 Derrick: And audit logs? We need full request traces retained for 12 months, immutable where possible.
01:29 Jordan: We have configurable audit log retention and can push logs to the customer's S3/bucket or to their logging pipeline. We'll capture token-level metadata but not raw outputs unless you opt in.
01:38 Sofia: On networking: transit-gateway is fine. Do you require VPC peering or do you support interface endpoints / privatelink?
01:44 Priya: We support both depending on cloud. For AWS: PrivateLink (interface endpoints) is our preferred path for private control-plane communications, but we can do VPC peering or transit-gateway attachments if that's your preferred topology. There's a tradeoff: PrivateLink avoids full routing, better for security, but requires ENIs and endpoint services. Peering can be simpler for certain topologies.
02:05 Sofia: Our compliance team hates extra ENIs in spokes — they'd prefer TGW attachments if possible. Also, we have overlapping CIDR ranges for a couple of legacy spokes, so full route propagation is tricky.
02:16 Priya: Overlapping CIDRs is common — in those cases we recommend using PrivateLink so routing tables aren't changed, or a NAT-based proxy. We can also host a small NAT/edge function in the hub to translate, but that's more of a lift.
02:28 Amir: Latency is a concern. Our SLO for inference is 120ms p95 for small models at 512 tokens. Any ballpark for added latency if we go through TGW + PrivateLink?
02:40 Priya: Typically the control-plane handshake is negligible; data plane depends on path. If the model is in the same region (eu-west-1) with private endpoints, you'd see single-digit ms for intra-region. TGW can add a few ms if traffic hops via hub. The bigger latency contributors are model queuing and cold KV cache misses. We can detail in the benchmark plan.
02:57 Jordan: We'll propose model placement: EU models pinned to eu nodes, and a routing policy that keeps EU requests local.
03:04 Sofia: What about egress — we cannot have calls to public endpoints, so no internet calls from the model hosts. How do you handle fetching model weights or updates?
03:12 Priya: For Private deployments, model images and artifacts are mirrored into your account or pulled from a private registry in your VPC. No outbound to public internet is required if you mirror images. Updates are pushed via an agreed pipeline.
03:23 Derrick: Any external telemetry? We need to ensure PII doesn't leave. Also, function-calling and tool integrations — do they require outbound?
03:31 Priya: Telemetry is optional. For PII-sensitive workloads, we recommend disabling telemetry and sending logs to your S3. Tool calls that invoke third-party services would only occur if you configure them; by default no external tool calls.
03:41 Amir: On identity, we use SAML SSO and want RBAC aligned to our AD groups. Can Redwood integrate with our identity provider for user auth in the console?
03:49 Jordan: Yes, SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning supported. For role-based access to models and routes we'll map roles to groups; Priya will show the mapping doc.
03:58 Priya: Also mention secrets: We can read KMS-backed secrets from your account; some customers prefer to inject creds using a secret-store mount.
04:07 Sofia: Ports — what ports need to be open from spokes to Redwood endpoints? We have strict ACLs.
04:12 Priya: We'll provide a minimal port list. Generally HTTPS over 443 for control-plane, and gRPC/HTTPS for data-plane depending on runtime — default is TLS on 443. If you use PrivateLink you'll need to allow the endpoint ENIs to talk to model subnets on specific ports; we'll send a hardened list.
04:29 Amir: Anything special for model caching? We do a lot of streaming telemetry and small messages, want to avoid cold starts.
04:35 Priya: We recommend continuous warmers and prefix caching. Our runtime supports KV caching for context windows. Note that some transcribers may call it \"KV cash\" in the notes, ha. Cache warmers are a config in the deployment.
04:47 Jordan: Competitor question: we heard Inferex supports on-prem GPUs directly; is Redwood able to run in air-gapped on-prem environments?
04:54 Priya: Yes — we have an on-prem package for air-gapped installs, but that comes with an enterprise licensing path and a design/ops engagement. For many customers, Private in their cloud VPC hits the compliance bar without full airgap.
05:05 Derrick: For KMS, we need HSM-backed key wrapping for model checkpoint encryption. Does that work?
05:11 Priya: If your KMS supports HSM (AWS KMS does for CMKs), we can use your CMKs for envelope encryption. We'll need key policies that allow the Redwood runtime role to decrypt; we can recommend the least privilege setup.
05:23 Sofia: Is there any network egress for metrics to Redwood console for monitoring?
05:27 Jordan: Observability data can be pushed to your cluster-level exporters; alternatively we can ship aggregated metrics to Redwood console over a control-plane endpoint. If you forbid outbound entirely, we support a push model where you push metrics into our secure endpoint over PrivateLink.
05:40 Amir: Ok procurement/ops — timeline is fast: want pilot in 6 weeks, production in 4 months if benchmarks look good. Success criteria: p95 latency under 120ms, 99% availability, and no EU egress.
05:55 Jordan: Noted. We'll layout pilot scope: small fleet of models in eu-west-1, sample traffic, and a runbook.
06:02 Priya: For the benchmark, we'll need sample payloads, concurrency targets, and any adversarial prompts. Also, we'll run perf with quantized models to meet cost/latency targets.
06:13 Amir: How do you handle fallbacks if a node is unavailable? We need graceful degradation, not complete outage.
06:19 Priya: We have smart routing and model variant fallbacks — requests can route to a smaller model or a different variant in region; we can configure automatic fallback with circuit-breakers.
06:29 Sofia: Certificate management — who rotates certs for PrivateLink endpoints?
06:33 Priya: We can use customer-managed certs from ACM, or Redwood can manage cert rotation. For strict compliance, customers usually provide certs via ACM in their account.
06:42 Derrick: A couple more sec items: we need penetration test windows and support for our pentest team to run blackbox tests against the endpoints.
06:48 Jordan: We support pentest engagements; we'll coordinate a window and an NDA.
06:52 Amir: Great. Anything we missed?
06:55 Sofia: Bandwidth — we'll need estimated egress and pricing implications if we handle huge token volumes.
06:59 Priya: We'll produce an estimated token-cost and network egress calc as part of the pilot proposal. Also batching/caching suggestions to reduce token counts.
07:10 Jordan: Ok quick recap — Jordan to send private deployment architecture and pricing, Priya to send benchmark plan and port list, Amir to share VPC CIDRs and compliance questionnaire. Pilot target ~6 weeks.
07:22 Amir: Sounds good, thanks.
07:25 Jordan: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with calendar invites for the kickoff and benchmark sync.

Notes on transcription: occasional words misheard (eg, \"KV cash\"), company names spelled OK overall. Recording ends.

03:31 Priya: Telemetry is optional. For PII-sensitive workloads, we recommend disabling telemetry and sending logs to your S3. Tool calls that invoke third-party services would only occur if you configure them; by default no external tool calls.
03:41 Amir: On identity, we use SAML SSO and want RBAC aligned to our AD groups. Can Redwood integrate with our identity provider for user auth in the console?
03:49 Jordan: Yes, SAML SSO + SCIM provisioning supported. For role-based access to models and routes we'll map roles to groups; Priya will show the mapping doc.

04:07 Sofia: Ports — what ports need to be open from spokes to Redwood endpoints? We have strict ACLs.

If you use PrivateLink you'll need to allow the endpoint ENIs to talk to model subnets on specific ports; we'll send a hardened list.

[12:45] Jordan Blake: Thanks all, we'll follow up with notes and calendar invites.

[12:52] -- meeting ended --

- Occasionally product names were mis-transcribed ("Redwood Privy" for "Redwood Private").

title: Amber Labs - capacity commitment & edge activation roadmap
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date: 2026-12-15
- Start: 14:00 PT
- Duration: ~43 minutes
- Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Maya Chen (Redwood SE), Diego Alvarez (Redwood CSM); Priya Kapoor (Amber Labs, Head of ML), Liam O'Donnell (CTO), Sofia Reyes (Infra)

Summary:
Quick renewal/expansion check: Amber Labs is evaluating a 12-month commitment to Redwood Dedicated with an EU private/on-prem pilot for latency-sensitive inference. Main drivers: data residency, lower token unit cost, and predictable tail latency. Customer wants timeline and pricing options, plus security confirmations for KMS/SSO and audit logs.

Topics covered:
- Current usage profile and pain points (latency spikes, KV cache misses)
- Proposed committed capacity and burst policy
- EU private / on-prem pilot scope and timeline
- Security/compliance checkpoints (SOC2, KMS, SSO, audit logs)
- Next steps and contract amendment cadence

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: Alright, hey everyone, thanks for carving out time. Uh this is Jordan from Redwood, I'm on the AE side, with Maya our SE, and Diego from CS. How's everyone doing?
[00:15] Priya: Good, thanks. We're eager to run through the details, we've been looking at the cost delta and the EU private option.
[00:40] Maya: Cool. Quick agenda - validate your production profile, propose committed tiers, then map a pilot for EU on-prem. We'll also call out security items you flagged. Sound good?
[01:05] Liam: Yep, that's perfect. We want to lock a contract that gives us predictability—bursting is important for weekends—and a clear path to on-prem in EU.
[02:18] Jordan: Excellent. Priya, can you remind us of current monthly tokens and peak qps? The sheet you shared said 1.4B tokens/month and peaks at about 120 qps right?
[02:40] Priya: Yeah, roughly. The 1.4B is across gen + embeddings. Peak qps for generate endpoints closer to 110-130 during batch jobs. We see latency outliers sometimes 500 to 900 ms, which is causing a few user-facing issues.
[05:02] Maya: Right, so two levers here: dedicated reserved capacity to reduce queuing and a regional/private deployment to cut network TTFB. Also caching improvements and KV cache warm starts can knock out the tail. We can give guidance on batch windows too.
[08:30] Sofia: Quick infra note: Our EU data center has strict network egress rules. For on-prem we need a whiteboard of what control plane connections are required. We can't have constant outbound to Redwood in some of our clusters.
[09:01] Maya: Understood. For Private we can do a customer-managed control plane model where the control plane is lightweight and only phones home for billing/healthcheck telemetry optionally. We also offer an air-gapped deployment for the stricter environments.
[11:12] Liam: What's the difference in SLA and cost between a dedicated hosted EU region versus full on-prem? Is the latency difference measurable?
[11:45] Jordan: Good question. Hosted dedicated in EU gives you our managed infra in a regional cloud with 99.9% SLA and lower ops overhead. On-prem private gives the best data residency and minimal network hops; latency improvements depend on co-location but expect 20-60% lower p95 depending on workload. Cost: on-prem includes an enterprise license plus install/support fee; dedicated is usage-based with reserved discounts. We'll send concrete numbers.
[14:20] Priya: For the cost model, we want a committed token price that reflects our 12-month burn estimate but also a burst bucket. Our CFO wants a hard cap on month-over-month cost increases.
[14:42] Jordan: We can do tiered committed pricing with a burst buffer at a predetermined premium; we can also add alerting thresholds and spend caps. We'll include a fixed CPI (cost per 1k tokens) for the committed portion and a higher CPI for burst.
[16:10] Diego: On the contract amendment, we typically do a short SOW appendix for private pilots that lays out duration, acceptance criteria, and rollback. That keeps the main contract clean and lets you try on-prem without long-term ops changes up front.
[18:55] Priya: Acceptance criteria - what does success look like? We want p95 under 300 ms for our main chat route and 95% reliability for the embedding pipeline during nightly batches.
[19:30] Maya: Agreed. We'll propose concrete SLIs: p95 latency target, KV cache hit ratio, and end-to-end error rate. Pilot acceptance will be measured on those.
[20:00] Liam: Security is another blocker. We need SOC2 documentation, SSO with our IdP (Okta), KMS integration (we use AWS KMS), and proof that audit logs are tamper-evident.
[21:10] Jordan: We have SOC2 Type II and can share the report under NDA. SSO via SAML/Okta is supported, and we can integrate with your AWS KMS for key management. Our audit logging supports syslog delivery or S3 bucket with immutability flags. We'll provide the security pack.
[22:35] Sofia: For the on-prem deployment, we need a step-by-step checklist for firewall rules and what ports must be opened. Even minimal outbound endpoints are a negotiation.
[23:05] Maya: I'll draft the runbook - ports, cert exchange, bootstrap steps, and rollback plan. We'll also note optional telemetry toggles.
[24:45] Priya: Timeline question — if we sign a 12-month commitment now, how fast can we be live in EU hosted dedicated, and then do an on-prem pilot?
[25:10] Jordan: Hosted dedicated EU - we can provision and route within 2-3 weeks. On-prem pilot - depends on provisioning on your side but typical time is 4-6 weeks for an initial pilot including security review.
[27:00] Priya: That aligns with our Q1 plan; target go-live for customer-facing features mid-Feb.
[28:10] Liam: One more thing — regression monitoring. If we change quantization or model variants during rollout, how do you ensure quality doesn't drop?
[28:45] Maya: We have Redwood Optimize suggestions + a canary rollout path. We can tie quality monitoring to your eval prompt sets and set regression alerts if embeddings or generation scores drop beyond thresholds.
[30:05] Sofia: Are there portability constraints? If we start on hosted and later migrate on-prem, how smooth is model variant parity and artifacts like KV cache?
[30:35] Maya: We design for portability. Model artifacts and kv caches are exportable; there are versioning semantics and a small compatibility test. We'll include an export/import playbook.
[33:05] Priya: On the commercial front, what are our options for a 12-month commitment with an optional 6-month trial for on-prem? CFO wants an exit clause if we don't meet p95 or cost SLOs during the pilot.
[34:00] Jordan: We can do a committed contract with an appendix that allows a pilot phase and specific termination terms tied to acceptance criteria. The key is to define measurable acceptance SLOs, and we can allow a partial refund or credit if not met within pilot scope.
[36:15] Liam: Competitors are pitching aggressive unit prices but lack the private on-prem story or the observability. Nimbus AI offered lower CPI but required a big lift on our side.
[37:20] Jordan: Right and Hyperserve doesn't offer the same KV cache tooling so they'd likely need more ops to hit your latency targets.
[38:50] Diego: Recap of concrete deliverables: Jordan sends pricing + draft amendment; Maya shares runbook and pilot SLOs; Priya/Sofia share workload artifacts and infra details.
[40:05] Priya: We'll get the prompt sets and infra inventory by Friday.
[41:00] Jordan: Great — shall we set a pilot kickoff next Tuesday? I'll send a calendar invite after the call.
[41:20] Priya: Works for us. Thanks everyone.
[42:30] Jordan: Thanks, we'll follow up with the docs and a proposed timeline. Bye.

End of transcript.

title: Novalink POC - concurrency orchestration pulse sync
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-10
Time: 16:00 PST (50m)
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Menon (Redwood SE), Liam O'Connor (CSM); Aisha Khan (Novalink Head of ML), Marco Ruiz (Platform), Dana Lee (CTO), Evan Park (SRE)

Summary: quick throughput pulse for week 4 of the POC. Reviewed steady-state numbers, saw a tail latency bump on small prompt paths when concurrency hit 1.2k, discussed continuous batching vs aggressive concurrency, validated KV-cache hit rates, agreed on a headroom run and config tweaks. Priya will push updated batch policy and Jordan to follow up on pricing.

[00:00] Jordan: hey everyone thanks for joining, can you hear me ok?
[00:06] Aisha: yes, loud and clear. Thanks. we just hopped off a platform standup so a little delayed.
[00:12] Jordan: no worries. quick agenda — numbers from the last 48 hours, queue/backpressure behavior, continuous batching adjustments, and next steps for the headroom test. Priya's gonna lead through the perf notes.
[00:28] Priya: hi team, thanks. So quick TL;DR — overall tokens/sec increased about 18% after we tuned the batch window, but small-request P95 spiked from 220ms to ~480ms during the mid-afternoon surge. We traced it to queue stacking on the small prompt route.
[00:46] Marco: just to be clear — small prompt path is the /api/v1/generate short prompt? that's what we saw, yes.
[00:52] Priya: yep. And I think the issue is our concurrency target was bumped heuristically to 1.2k which allowed larger batches to saturate bytes and cause queuing for low-latency requests. continuous batching gives you efficiency but can hide latency for smalls if not shaped.
[01:12] Evan: quick question — is this the K V cache cold miss problem or more of the batching window aligning?
[01:18] Priya: mostly batching alignment and backlog. KV cache hit rates are decent — around 86% for the session patterns we saw. There were a few cold-start tails at request bursts, but the big driver was queue depth at the aggregator layer.
[01:34] Dana: curious — when you say aggregator layer, do you mean the request router in Redwood Dedicaed?
[01:40] Priya: sorry, Redwood Dedicated — yes the routing tier. We had an earlier typo in the instrumentation where the route name was trimmed so some traces didn't attribute correctly.
[01:50] Jordan: important note: this POC uses hosted runtime with reserved burst. we did an ad-hoc fallback to a smaller model at 80% CPU which avoided outages.
[01:58] Aisha: I saw that fallback in the dashboard — nice. but we still want predictable P95 for smalls under 500ms.
[02:06] Priya: agreed. So proposed changes: 1) lower max concurrency for the small prompt route to 700, 2) enable priority queueing (small requests higher priority), 3) reduce batch-accumulate window from 12ms to 6ms for that route, 4) pre-warm KV cache for known session ids.
[02:28] Marco: lowering concurrency seems like it reduces throughput. won't that force us to buy more GPU hours?
[02:32] Priya: there's a tradeoff — you lose some efficiency per-GPU, but the net user-perceived latency improves. Also we can use targeted autoscale bursts during known traffic spikes which helps. Jordan has cost scenarios.
[02:46] Jordan: yes i'll send a table with delta cost per qps after these knobs. We also have quantization suggestions that can give 10-20% throughput improvement without latency regressions for small prompts.
[02:58] Aisha: can you unpack the quantization idea? earlier tests showed slight quality drift.
[03:04] Priya: sure — we recommended int8-quant for the llm variant you're running for non-critical completions. For the high-fidelity clinical narratives we keep float16. So route-tiering by model-variant is another lever.
[03:18] Evan: what about KV-cache capacity? We saw rejections around 11:20am last Tuesday — can we increase cache capacity?
[03:24] Priya: yes, increasing xslots in KV helps; also spreading session keys across more shards reduces hotspotting. We applied a temporary increase last run which improved hit rate from 79% to 86%.
[03:36] Dana: ok, what do you need from us to run the headroom test?
[03:40] Priya: we need a tagged load run from Novalink that simulates 10k small concurrent requests over a 10 minute window. tag header X-NVLK-Test:headroom so we can apply the priority rules in the router and capture traces. Also please keep sample ratio 1% for full traces.
[03:56] Aisha: we can schedule that Thursday morning. Marco will run it from our k8s cluster. question — will the test affect production?
[04:04] Jordan: we'll route the load to dedicated pilot cluster, so no impact on prod. still, don't send real PHI in payloads — use synthetic prompts.
[04:12] Marco: noted. We'll prepare the test job and request tagging.
[04:16] Priya: great — after the run we'll analyze queue histograms, batch size distribution, and KV hit timeline and share a playback.
[04:24] Liam: quick ops note — we also recommend enabling request-level timeouts at 2.5s to bound tail behavior during tests.
[04:30] Marco: ok we'll add that to the job config.
[04:34] [05:00] Jordan: moving on to measured numbers — Priya do you want to walk the spreadsheet? (link shared in chat: https://redwood.app/poctest/novalink/week4)
[05:06] Priya: yep. So main panel — steady token throughput went from 3200 t/s to 3780 t/s, aggregate batching efficiency went from 1.8 to 2.2 tokens per ms. However P95 smalls moved as I said earlier. You can see the timestamped spike around 11:18 where concurrency jumped.
[05:30] Aisha: I see the green and orange lines — what are those exactly?
[05:34] Priya: green is small-route P50/P95; orange is large-prompt P95. The orange stayed flat which suggests the large prompts benefit from packing; it's the smalls that got queued behind those larger batches.
[05:48] Dana: can we do a second queue for smalls only? I mean dedicated micro-queue consumers.
[05:54] Priya: yes that's the priority queue idea. We can pin a subset of workers to service priority queue so small requests don't wait behind big batches. That is slightly less efficient per-worker but gives latency isolation.
[06:06] Evan: what's the expected uplift on P95 if we do that? ballpark.
[06:10] Priya: testing shows P95 for smalls drops from ~480ms to ~220-260ms in our sims when priority consumers are enabled and concurrency limited.
[06:20] Jordan: we should also call out a hybrid — keep continuous batching on but hard-limit wait window and enforce priority consumers for <128-token requests.
[06:28] Marco: how hard is that to implement in our environment? we use envoy as edge, then the request router, then the aggregator — can we tag on envoy?
[06:36] Priya: yes you can tag in envoy or in the app layer. We added a simple request header rule in the demo. We'll share the envoy snippet.
[06:44] Aisha: good. any other anomalies in traces?
[06:48] Priya: one more — there's a small GC pause on the aggregator node at 11:19 which increased queue backlog briefly; we flagged it but it wasn't the primary cause. we recommended moving aggregator GC to off-peak or bumping memory.
[07:00] Dana: okay let's do memory bump for aggregator in the next run. Marco can you handle?
[07:04] Marco: yes, will change and redeploy.
[07:06] [07:30] Jordan: commercial note — you're close to the throughput tiers where dedicated makes more sense. The mix of priority consumers plus reduced concurrency maybe pushes cost up a bit but stable latency for clinicians is worth it.
[07:40] Aisha: we'll review cost scenarios. Jordan you mentioned price with burst capacity — can you clarify burst SLA?
[07:46] Jordan: burst is best-effort with pre-warmed GPUs; there's a small premium but you get capacity for short windows. We'll show price per token across steady and burst.
[07:56] Priya: ok next — config changes we plan to apply today in the pilot cluster (non-prod) are: reduce small-route concurrency to 700, set batch-accumulate to 6ms for that route, enable priority queue and spin up 2 priority consumer pods, increase KV slots by 20%, and bump aggregator memory from 12GB to 20GB.
[08:18] Evan: will that need a restart?
[08:20] Priya: some will, some are dynamic. We'll schedule a rolling restart for aggregator and the priority consumers will be added dynamically. We'll post a runbook in the shared doc.
[08:30] Aisha: would you also add a quick note about telemetry tags? we want trace ids in our SIEM.
[08:34] Priya: yes we'll include trace id propagation and sample ratio in the runbook.
[08:38] Jordan: any risks? Priya?
[08:40] Priya: risk is slightly lower batch efficiency and hence cost, also model-variant split operational complexity. But mitigations are clear.
[08:48] Dana: ok. about security/compliance — we still need the SOC2 docs. Jordan, you sent something last month, can we get the updated SOC2 + KMS details?
[08:58] Jordan: yes i'll re-share the latest security whitepaper and KMS overview. Expect in email today.
[09:04] Marco: on encryption — are session keys stored in cloud KMS or customer-managed?
[09:10] Jordan: currently we default to Redwood-managed KMS for the hosted trial but Private deployments support customer-managed keys/HSM. We'll surface options in the doc.
[09:20] Aisha: sounds good. any competitor traps we should be aware of — we've had sales outreach from Anthropic about dedicated endpoints.
[09:28] Jordan: yeah that's expected. Our diff is the tooling for ops (KV cache tuning, priority queueing, continuous batching controls) and the console traces. You'll see better observability for queue shaping.
[09:38] Priya: final note — we'll also run a micro-benchmark that isolates batch packing overhead to quantify how much of the P95 is batching vs model compute. That will help decide quantization.
[09:50] Aisha: great. schedule the headroom run Thurs and we'll sync on Friday?
[09:56] Jordan: Friday sync works. We'll aim 10am PT.
[10:00] Liam: recap of next steps and owners — Priya share batch policy and envoy snippet, Marco run headroom with tagging, Aisha upload synthetic prompts and ensure no PHI, Jordan send pricing and SOC2 docs.
[10:12] Dana: thanks all.
[10:14] Jordan: thanks everyone, chat thread will have the links and action items. bye.

End of transcript.

[03:04] Priya: sure — we recommended int8-quant for the llm variant you're running for non-critical completions. For the high-fidelity clinical narratives we keep float16. So route-tiering by model-variant is another lever.
[03:18] Evan: what about KV-cache capacity? We saw rejections around 11:20am last Tuesday — can we increase cache capacity?
[03:24] Priya: yes, increasing xslots in KV helps; also spreading session keys across more shards reduces hotspotting. We applied a temporary increase last run which improved hit rate from 79% to 86%.
[03:36] Dana: ok, what do you need from us to run the headroom test?
[03:40] Priya: we need a tagged load run from Novalink that simulates 10k small concurrent requests over a 10 minute window. tag header X-NVLK-Test:headroom so we can apply the priority rules in the router and capture traces. Also please keep sample ratio 1% for full traces.
[03:56] Aisha: we can schedule that Thursday morning. Marco will run it from our k8s cluster. question — will the test affect production?
[04:04] Jordan: we'll route the load to dedicated pilot cluster, so no impact on prod. still, don't send real PHI in payloads — use synthetic prompts.
[04:12] Marco: noted. We'll prepare the test job and request tagging.
[04:16] Priya: great — after the run we'll analyze queue histograms, batch size distribution, and KV hit timeline and share a playback.
[04:24] Liam: quick ops note — we also recommend enabling request-level timeouts at 2.5s to bound tail behavior during tests.
[04:30] Marco: ok we'll add that to the job config.
[04:34] [05:00] Jordan: moving on to measured numbers — Priya do you want to walk the spreadsheet? (link shared in chat: https://redwood.app/poctest/novalink/week4)
[05:06] Priya: yep. So main panel — steady token throughput went from 3200 t/s to 3780 t/s, aggregate batching efficiency went from 1.8 to 2.2 tokens per ms. However P95 smalls moved as I said earlier. You can see the timestamped spike around 11:18 where concurrency jumped.
[05:30] Aisha: I see the green and orange lines — what are those exactly?
[05:34] Priya: green is small-route P50/P95; orange is large-prompt P95. The orange stayed flat which suggests the large prompts benefit from packing; it's the smalls that got queued behind those larger batches.
[05:48] Dana: can we do a second queue for smalls only? I mean dedicated micro-queue consumers.
[05:54] Priya: yes that's the priority queue idea. We can pin a subset of workers to service priority queue so small requests don't wait behind big batches. That is slightly less efficient per-worker but gives latency isolation.
[06:06] Evan: what's the expected uplift on P95 if we do that? ballpark.
[06:10] Priya: testing shows P95 for smalls drops from ~480ms to ~220-260ms in our sims when priority consumers are enabled and concurrency limited.
[06:20] Jordan: we should also call out a hybrid — keep continuous batching on but hard-limit wait window and enforce priority consumers for <128-token requests.
[06:28] Marco: how hard is that to implement in our environment? we use envoy as edge, then the request router, then the aggregator — can we tag on envoy?
[06:36] Priya: yes you can tag in envoy or in the app layer. We added a simple request header rule in the demo. We'll share the envoy snippet.
[06:44] Aisha: good. any other anomalies in traces?
[06:48] Priya: one more — there's a small GC pause on the aggregator node at 11:19 which increased queue backlog briefly; we flagged it but it wasn't the primary cause. we recommended moving aggregator GC to off-peak or bumping memory.
[07:00] Dana: okay let's do memory bump for aggregator in the next run. Marco can you handle?
[07:04] Marco: yes, will change and redeploy.
[07:06] [07:30] Jordan: commercial note — you're close to the throughput tiers where dedicated makes more sense. The mix of priority consumers plus reduced concurrency maybe pushes cost up a bit but stable latency for clinicians is worth it.
[07:40] Aisha: we'll review cost scenarios. Jordan you mentioned price with burst capacity — can you clarify burst SLA?
[07:46] Jordan: burst is best-effort with pre-warmed GPUs; there's a small premium but you get capacity for short windows. We'll show price per token across steady and burst.
[07:56] Priya: ok next — config changes we plan to apply today in the pilot cluster (non-prod) are: reduce small-route concurrency to 700, set batch-accumulate to 6ms for that route, enable priority queue and spin up 2 priority consumer pods, increase KV slots by 20%, and bump aggregator memory from 12GB to 20GB.
[08:18] Evan: will that need a restart?
[08:20] Priya: some will, some are dynamic. We'll schedule a rolling restart for aggregator and the priority consumers will be added dynamically. We'll post a runbook in the shared doc.
[08:30] Aisha: would you also add a quick note about telemetry tags? we want trace ids in our SIEM.
[08:34] Priya: yes we'll include trace id propagation and sample ratio in the runbook.
[08:38] Jordan: any risks? Priya?
[08:40] Priya: risk is slightly lower batch efficiency and hence cost, also model-variant split operational complexity. But mitigations are clear.
[08:48] Dana: ok. about security/compliance — we still need the SOC2 docs. Jordan, you sent something last month, can we get the updated SOC2 + KMS details?
[08:58] Jordan: yes i'll re-share the latest security whitepaper and KMS overview. Expect in email today.
[09:04] Marco: on encryption — are session keys stored in cloud KMS or customer-managed?
[09:10] Jordan: currently we default to Redwood-managed KMS for the hosted trial but Private deployments support customer-managed keys/HSM. We'll surface options in the doc.
[09:20] Aisha: sounds good. any competitor traps we should be aware of — we've had sales outreach from Anthropic about dedicated endpoints.
[09:28] Jordan: yeah that's expected. Our diff is the tooling for ops (KV cache tuning, priority queueing, continuous batching controls) and the console traces. You'll see better observability for queue shaping.
[09:38] Priya: final note — we'll also run a micro-benchmark that isolates batch packing overhead to quantify how much of the P95 is batching vs model compute. That will help decide quantization.
[09:50] Aisha: great. schedule the headroom run Thurs and we'll sync on Friday?
[09:56] Jordan: Friday sync works. We'll aim 10am PT.
[10:00] Liam: recap of next steps and owners — Priya share batch policy and envoy snippet, Marco run headroom with tagging, Aisha upload synthetic prompts and ensure no PHI, Jordan send pricing and SOC2 docs.
[10:12] Dana: thanks all.
[10:14] Jordan: thanks everyone, chat thread will have the links and action items. bye.

End of transcript.
