title: Proxy + LLM Adapter Integration - ScoutWorks Discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-11-05
Time: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Nair (Redwood SE), Omar Ruiz (Redwood Solutions), Liam Chen (CTO - ScoutWorks), Marisol Vega (Platform Engineering), Tomas Rivera (DevOps), Aisha Patel (Security)

Summary: quick discovery to understand ScoutWorks' plan to integrate LLMs into a document processing pipeline; primary concerns around hosted vs dedicated vs private deployments, gateway/proxy architecture with an edge adapter, LangChain/LlamaIndex SDK compatibility, rate limiting and quota model, and observability hooks (tracing, token-level cost). Customer wants a clear path to prototype with minimal data residency exposure and an eventual private deployment behind their VPC.

Topics: integration architecture, SDK adapters, LangChain / LlamaIndex examples, rate limits/quotas, caching and KV cache behavior, observability/tracing, security (KMS, SOC2, SSO), rollout strategy and benchmarking.

00:00:08 Jordan Blake: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick intro from our side, I'm Jordan, AE at Redwood, and with me is Priya, our solutions engineer, and Omar on solutions. Would you like to do intros from the ScoutWorks side?
00:00:20 Liam Chen: Sure, I'm Liam, CTO. we've been prototyping a doc ingestion pipeline and we need inference at scale for summarization and question answering.
00:00:27 Marisol Vega: Marisol, platform engineering. I'll be owning the integration and infra.
00:00:31 Tomás Rivera: Tomas, DevOps. I'll look at deployment, network, and our current gateway.
00:00:34 Aisha Patel: Aisha, security. I'll be asking about compliance and encryption.
00:00:40 Jordan Blake: Great. high level, can you give us your current stack and the expected QPS/latency targets?
00:00:46 Liam Chen: Right now it's a lambda-based ingest then a small microservice that fans out to workers. For 6 months we expect 200 to 600 requests per minute, p95 latency target for interactive flows is <800ms end-to-end. For batch, it's okay to be higher. We're also very concerned about cost per token, so we want techniques to control spend.
00:01:05 Priya Nair: Makes sense. are you currently using any LLM SDKs or orchestration frameworks like LangChain or LlamaIndex?
00:01:11 Marisol Vega: We have a short POC using LangChain for RAG. LlamaIndex we tried in a demo but not in production. We also have a Kong gateway fronting APIs right now.
00:01:21 Omar Ruiz: And do you need the Redwood SDK to sit behind your gateway, or are you thinking Redwood as the upstream model provider?
00:01:28 Marisol Vega: Ideally Redwood is upstream. We want to keep our gateway as a policy layer for auth and rate-limiting, but want flexibility to run either Hosted for dev and Dedicated or Private later for production.
00:01:38 Priya Nair: Okay, so a hybrid path: start Hosted for quick iteration, then Dedicated reserved capacity or Private on VPC for compliance. We often recommend using our SDK inside your services or via a small adapter layer in the gateway for centralized request shaping and batching.
00:01:52 Tomás Rivera: If we put the SDK in the gateway, what's the impact on connection counts and KV cache? we want to avoid blowing out memory on edge nodes.
00:01:59 Priya Nair: Good question. Our runtime keeps a per-connection KV cache on the serving side; the SDK itself is lightweight. For gateway adapters we recommend a sidecar or a dedicated worker pool for inference so the gateway stays stateless. That way the KV cache is associated with the worker lifecycle. Also batching is typically handled at the worker to hit throughput targets without increasing latency for single requests.
00:02:23 Liam Chen: And how easy is it to plug in LangChain or LlamaIndex? we have some prompt templates and chains already.
00:02:28 Priya Nair: We have SDK examples for both LangChain and LlamaIndex. Priya: (laughs) I can drop a snippet in the repo. For LangChain, you can implement a custom LLM wrapper that calls Redwood's text generation endpoint. For LlamaIndex, the node/python connectors can call Redwood or a proxy adapter. We also have examples for function calling and structured responses.
00:02:50 Marisol Vega: Do you support streaming? that affects how we implement the UI.
00:02:53 Omar Ruiz: Yes, streaming is supported. with Hosted we stream SSE/WebSocket; for Private you can enable streaming over your vpc peering and we stream tokens incrementally. Note: customers sometimes see intermittent token duplication in cheap transcriptions - it's a small transcription artifact.
00:03:06 Aisha Patel: On security, do you have SOC 2 and how does KMS integration look for Private deployments?
00:03:12 Priya Nair: Redwood is SOC 2 Type II. For Private deployments we integrate with customer KMS/HSM via key wrap and you can bring your own key. We also support customer-managed encryption of logs and you can configure audit log retention. We'll send the security pack after this call.
00:03:27 Tomás Rivera: Network wise, we'd prefer a VPC peering model, or is there an option for an on-prem air-gapped package?
00:03:34 Jordan Blake: Both. VPC peering and private deployment options exist. air-gapped on-prem is possible for large enterprise customers but includes a longer onboarding. For many customers Dedicated within a cloud region with private networking hits the compliance bar.
00:03:48 Liam Chen: Rate limits — we need fine-grained quotas per team and per route. can we define token budgets per API key and enforce burst policies?
00:03:57 Priya Nair: Yes, our rate limit system supports per-token quotas, per-route and per-api-key caps, and burst windows. We can also surface quota usage in the console and via webhooks to your SRE systems. There's an optional gateway plugin to enforce limits at the edge if you want to keep enforcement in your Kong layer.
00:04:18 Marisol Vega: How does caching work for repeated prompts or embeddings? sharding across models?
00:04:23 Omar Ruiz: We support prefix and KV cache for autoregressive models to reuse previous context and we have embedding caching for identical vectors or exact-match payloads. For scale, cache is sharded by customer and model variant to avoid cross-tenant leaks. Note: exact-match dedupe works best when you pass normalized payloads.
00:04:41 Liam Chen: Observability is huge for us. can you give detail on traces, token-level cost, and integration with our existing observability stack (we're on DataDog + Honeycomb)?
00:04:51 Priya Nair: We emit tracing spans and you can get per-request token counts. Redwood Console surface per-route latency and token breakdowns. We have a tracing hook that can push spans to OTLP collectors. For Datadog/Honeycomb we supply an exporter example. We'll share a short arch diagram and a sample tracing config.
00:05:09 Aisha Patel: What about PII and log retention?
00:05:12 Jordan Blake: By default hosted logs are retained 30 days and redacted for PII where possible; for Dedicated and Private you can control retention and disable logging of raw payloads. For strict compliance you should go Private or Dedicated with customer-managed logging.
00:05:24 Marisol Vega: Can you walk through a concrete prototyping plan? we'd like to get a proof-of-concept in 2 weeks if possible.
00:05:30 Jordan Blake: Typical plan: (1) Sandbox Hosted account + credits — same API; (2) Priya drops LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter code and an SDK snippet for the gateway; (3) ScoutWorks run smoke tests with a 1k-doc subset; (4) 1-hour perf benchmark with your traces to estimate Dedicated sizing; (5) Decide on Dedicated vs Private after compliance review. Does that map?
00:05:49 Liam Chen: Yes. For the perf benchmark, we can share one week of anonymized traces. Tomas will prepare that.
00:05:56 Tomás Rivera: Quick question on model variants and fallback — if a Dedicated node is overloaded can we fallback to Hosted?
00:06:03 Priya Nair: Yes, smart routing can fallback to a compatible variant in Hosted if you enable cross-tier fallback. We recommend setting budget-aware routing to control cost. Fallbacks preserve API surface but may have different latency and cost. There can be slight differences in tokenizer behavior across model builds.
00:06:20 Marisol Vega: Folllow-up on quotas: what happens if a team goes over token budget mid-day; can we auto-throttle or send webhooks?
00:06:27 Omar Ruiz: We support webhooks and threshold alerts. Auto-throttling options exist but we usually recommend soft-limits + alerts + enforced hard limit to avoid in-flight failures. Throttling behavior is configurable per-project.
00:06:40 Liam Chen: For our RAG flow with LangChain, embeddings cost is a major line item. do you have batching/caching suggestions specific to embeddings?
00:06:48 Priya Nair: For embeddings, batch requests into groups of 64-256 depending on vector size, and use embedding cache with TTL for near-dedup. Also pre-filter candidates to reduce calls. Redwood Optimize can analyze your workload and suggest quantization or a cheaper model variant for embeddings.
00:07:06 Tomás Rivera: If we run Dedicated, how does autoscaling work and what is the expected cold-start time for new nodes?
00:07:13 Jordan Blake: Dedicated pools have configurable autoscaling and burst profiles. New GPU nodes tend to warm up in a few minutes; however KV cache hit rates ramp as context warms. For very spiky workloads we recommend a buffer of baseline capacity to avoid latency cliffs.
00:07:26 Aisha Patel: On the Private option - do you support SAML/SSO and SCIM for user provisioning?
00:07:31 Priya Nair: Yes, SAML/SSO, SCIM provisioning, RBAC, and audit trails are supported. For Private, you can integrate with your IdP directly.
00:07:38 Liam Chen: Any gotchas with LlamaIndex connectors? we saw tokenization mismatches in another POC.
00:07:44 Priya Nair: Tokenization differences can happen across model builds. LlamaIndex connectors should normalize text and handle tokenization on the client side; we recommend passing a token budget in the wrapper and using the SDK to calculate tokens before send for consistent billing.
00:07:58 Marisol Vega: can you show an SDK snippet for a LangChain wrapper?
00:08:02 Priya Nair: Sure I'll paste a short sample in the follow-up email but high level: implement LLM class that calls Redwood's /v1/generate, handle streaming tokens by yielding generator to LangChain, and include a retry/backoff strategy. For batching, use a shared worker that accumulates requests and calls the batch API.
00:08:21 Jordan Blake: I'm seeing a question in chat from Tomas about quota enforcement at the gateway. Tomas, do you want enforcement at Kong or at Redwood?
00:08:28 Tomás Rivera: Preferably Kong for early rejection, but if we need centralized visibility Redwood enforcement is fine.
00:08:33 Omar Ruiz: To reconcile both, we recommend edge enforcement for immediate rejection and Redwood for final accounting. We have a plugin blueprint for Kong that queries Redwood token counters and allows fast local rejects for obvious overages.
00:08:48 Liam Chen: What about canary rollouts and A/B testing models? we want to test a cheaper quantized variant vs a high-quality one.
00:08:56 Priya Nair: The Console supports weighted routing for rollouts and you can drive percent traffic to model variants. Redwood Optimize can automatically suggest cheaper quantized variants and we provide evaluation harnesses to compare quality metrics. We also emit evaluation hooks to feed back into your QA.
00:09:12 Marisol Vega: Are there shared artifacts we can look at after this?
00:09:15 Jordan Blake: Yes, we'll share: security deck (SOC2 and KMS notes), SDK LangChain + LlamaIndex snippets repo link, an architecture sketch for gateway adapter, and a perf spreadsheet template.
00:09:25 Aisha Patel: Please include an ILM for logs in the security deck.
00:09:29 Jordan Blake: Noted.
00:09:31 Priya Nair: Quick technical note — when you run the adapter, make sure you pin model versions. Rolling model versions can cause subtle output drift in RAG chains.
00:09:41 Tomas Rivera: pinned versions ok. Also, we need to know about token billing windows. do you bill per request or per minute averages?
00:09:48 Jordan Blake: Billing is per-token consumed for Hosted. For Dedicated we have committed monthly units and per-token accounting for overages. The console shows per-token across routes and invoicing aligns to monthly buckets.
00:09:59 Liam Chen: Pricing bands? you said you'll send those. want something for our Finance team.
00:10:03 Jordan Blake: Yes, we'll include Hosted pay-as-you-go, Dedicated reserved tiers with example cost-per-token, and Private deployment licensing.
00:10:11 Marisol Vega: Any latency numbers we can expect for a typical 8k context window on Dedicated?
00:10:17 Priya Nair: For an 8k window on a Dedicated cluster with the right kernel optimizations, p95 generation latency for single-turn is often 200-500ms depending on prompt complexity. Bursty traffic and cold KV cache will be higher. We'll run the perf bench to show exact numbers with your payload.
00:10:34 Liam Chen: Great. moving to the final 10 minutes — are there specific blockers we foresee?
00:10:38 Aisha Patel: From security, PII retention and KMS integration are the main blockers. Need to see SOC2/Security docs.
00:10:45 Tomás Rivera: From infra, we need the Kong plugin for local quota enforcement and assurance that the adapter won't inflate connection counts.
00:10:53 Marisol Vega: For platform, examples for LangChain and LlamaIndex and streaming behavior in UI.
00:10:59 Jordan Blake: Action items recap: Priya will send SDK examples and LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter; Jordan will send pricing and security deck; Tomas will upload traces for perf. We'll schedule a 60-minute perf bench next week. Any final Qs?
00:11:14 Liam Chen: No, that covers it. thanks everyone.
00:11:18 Jordan Blake: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the links.

[End of recording — meeting lasted 52 minutes. The transcript contains some automated punctuation fixes; occasional mis-transcripts present. Shared artifacts during call: https://redwood.ai/security-deck (placeholder), https://github.com/redwood-inference/langchain-examples (placeholder), "ScoutWorks - perf template" spreadsheet (link shared in follow-up).

Recap & next steps:
- Redwood to provide Hosted sandbox creds and example repo for LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter.
- ScoutWorks to share anonymized traces and token distribution for a 1-week window.
- Schedule perf bench (Priya + Tomas + Liam) — target next week.
- Jordan to share Dedicated pricing bands and Private deployment checklist.
- Priya to include Kong plugin blueprint and Datadog/Honeycomb OTLP hook examples.

Action item quick list:
- Priya Nair: Send LangChain + LlamaIndex SDK adapter snippets & Kong plugin blueprint by 2026-11-10.
- Jordan Blake: Send pricing bands + security deck link by 2026-11-09.
- Tomás Rivera (ScoutWorks): Upload anonymized traces + token distribution by 2026-11-12.
- Aisha Patel (ScoutWorks): Send security constraints and required certifications list by 2026-11-13.

End.

00:05:24 Marisol Vega: Can you walk through a concrete prototyping plan? we'd like to get a proof-of-concept in 2 weeks if possible.
00:05:30 Jordan Blake: Typical plan: (1) Sandbox Hosted account + credits — same API; (2) Priya drops LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter code and an SDK snippet for the gateway; (3) ScoutWorks run smoke tests with a 1k-doc subset; (4) 1-hour perf benchmark with your traces to estimate Dedicated sizing; (5) Decide on Dedicated vs Private after compliance review. Does that map?
00:05:49 Liam Chen: Yes. For the perf benchmark, we can share one week of anonymized traces. Tomas will prepare that.
00:05:56 Tomás Rivera: Quick question on model variants and fallback — if a Dedicated node is overloaded can we fallback to Hosted?
00:06:03 Priya Nair: Yes, smart routing can fallback to a compatible variant in Hosted if you enable cross-tier fallback. We recommend setting budget-aware routing to control cost. Fallbacks preserve API surface but may have different latency and cost. There can be slight differences in tokenizer behavior across model builds.
00:06:20 Marisol Vega: Folllow-up on quotas: what happens if a team goes over token budget mid-day; can we auto-throttle or send webhooks?
00:06:27 Omar Ruiz: We support webhooks and threshold alerts. Auto-throttling options exist but we usually recommend soft-limits + alerts + enforced hard limit to avoid in-flight failures. Throttling behavior is configurable per-project.
00:06:40 Liam Chen: For our RAG flow with LangChain, embeddings cost is a major line item. do you have batching/caching suggestions specific to embeddings?
00:06:48 Priya Nair: For embeddings, batch requests into groups of 64-256 depending on vector size, and use embedding cache with TTL for near-dedup. Also pre-filter candidates to reduce calls. Redwood Optimize can analyze your workload and suggest quantization or a cheaper model variant for embeddings.
00:07:06 Tomás Rivera: If we run Dedicated, how does autoscaling work and what is the expected cold-start time for new nodes?
00:07:13 Jordan Blake: Dedicated pools have configurable autoscaling and burst profiles. New GPU nodes tend to warm up in a few minutes; however KV cache hit rates ramp as context warms. For very spiky workloads we recommend a buffer of baseline capacity to avoid latency cliffs.
00:07:26 Aisha Patel: On the Private option - do you support SAML/SSO and SCIM for user provisioning?
00:07:31 Priya Nair: Yes, SAML/SSO, SCIM provisioning, RBAC, and audit trails are supported. For Private, you can integrate with your IdP directly.
00:07:38 Liam Chen: Any gotchas with LlamaIndex connectors? we saw tokenization mismatches in another POC.
00:07:44 Priya Nair: Tokenization differences can happen across model builds. LlamaIndex connectors should normalize text and handle tokenization on the client side; we recommend passing a token budget in the wrapper and using the SDK to calculate tokens before send for consistent billing.
00:07:58 Marisol Vega: can you show an SDK snippet for a LangChain wrapper?
00:08:02 Priya Nair: Sure I'll paste a short sample in the follow-up email but high level: implement LLM class that calls Redwood's /v1/generate, handle streaming tokens by yielding generator to LangChain, and include a retry/backoff strategy. For batching, use a shared worker that accumulates requests and calls the batch API.
00:08:21 Jordan Blake: I'm seeing a question in chat from Tomas about quota enforcement at the gateway. Tomas, do you want enforcement at Kong or at Redwood?
00:08:28 Tomás Rivera: Preferably Kong for early rejection, but if we need centralized visibility Redwood enforcement is fine.
00:08:33 Omar Ruiz: To reconcile both, we recommend edge enforcement for immediate rejection and Redwood for final accounting. We have a plugin blueprint for Kong that queries Redwood token counters and allows fast local rejects for obvious overages.
00:08:48 Liam Chen: What about canary rollouts and A/B testing models? we want to test a cheaper quantized variant vs a high-quality one.
00:08:56 Priya Nair: The Console supports weighted routing for rollouts and you can drive percent traffic to model variants. Redwood Optimize can automatically suggest cheaper quantized variants and we provide evaluation harnesses to compare quality metrics. We also emit evaluation hooks to feed back into your QA.
00:09:12 Marisol Vega: Are there shared artifacts we can look at after this?
00:09:15 Jordan Blake: Yes, we'll share: security deck (SOC2 and KMS notes), SDK LangChain + LlamaIndex snippets repo link, an architecture sketch for gateway adapter, and a perf spreadsheet template.
00:09:25 Aisha Patel: Please include an ILM for logs in the security deck.
00:09:29 Jordan Blake: Noted.
00:09:31 Priya Nair: Quick technical note — when you run the adapter, make sure you pin model versions. Rolling model versions can cause subtle output drift in RAG chains.
00:09:41 Tomas Rivera: pinned versions ok. Also, we need to know about token billing windows. do you bill per request or per minute averages?
00:09:48 Jordan Blake: Billing is per-token consumed for Hosted. For Dedicated we have committed monthly units and per-token accounting for overages. The console shows per-token across routes and invoicing aligns to monthly buckets.
00:09:59 Liam Chen: Pricing bands? you said you'll send those. want something for our Finance team.
00:10:03 Jordan Blake: Yes, we'll include Hosted pay-as-you-go, Dedicated reserved tiers with example cost-per-token, and Private deployment licensing.
00:10:11 Marisol Vega: Any latency numbers we can expect for a typical 8k context window on Dedicated?
00:10:17 Priya Nair: For an 8k window on a Dedicated cluster with the right kernel optimizations, p95 generation latency for single-turn is often 200-500ms depending on prompt complexity. Bursty traffic and cold KV cache will be higher. We'll run the perf bench to show exact numbers with your payload.
00:10:34 Liam Chen: Great. moving to the final 10 minutes — are there specific blockers we foresee?
00:10:38 Aisha Patel: From security, PII retention and KMS integration are the main blockers. Need to see SOC2/Security docs.
00:10:45 Tomás Rivera: From infra, we need the Kong plugin for local quota enforcement and assurance that the adapter won't inflate connection counts.
00:10:53 Marisol Vega: For platform, examples for LangChain and LlamaIndex and streaming behavior in UI.
00:10:59 Jordan Blake: Action items recap: Priya will send SDK examples and LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter; Jordan will send pricing and security deck; Tomas will upload traces for perf. We'll schedule a 60-minute perf bench next week. Any final Qs?
00:11:14 Liam Chen: No, that covers it. thanks everyone.
00:11:18 Jordan Blake: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the links.

[End of recording — meeting lasted 52 minutes. The transcript contains some automated punctuation fixes; occasional mis-transcripts present. Shared artifacts during call: https://redwood.ai/security-deck (placeholder), https://github.com/redwood-inference/langchain-examples (placeholder), "ScoutWorks - perf template" spreadsheet (link shared in follow-up).

Recap & next steps:
- Redwood to provide Hosted sandbox creds and example repo for LangChain/LlamaIndex adapter.
- ScoutWorks to share anonymized traces and token distribution for a 1-week window.
- Schedule perf bench (Priya + Tomas + Liam) — target next week.
- Jordan to share Dedicated pricing bands and Private deployment checklist.
- Priya to include Kong plugin blueprint and Datadog/Honeycomb OTLP hook examples.

Action item quick list:
- Priya Nair: Send LangChain + LlamaIndex SDK adapter snippets & Kong plugin blueprint by 2026-11-10.
- Jordan Blake: Send pricing bands + security deck link by 2026-11-09.
- Tomás Rivera (ScoutWorks): Upload anonymized traces + token distribution by 2026-11-12.

End.

title: POC operability and onboarding checkpoint
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-10-07
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~47 mins
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Luis (Redwood CSM), Evan (Nexa ML), Marisa (Nexa SRE), Samir (Nexa Security), Ava (Nexa PM)
Link to notes: https://drive.redwood/example-poc-notes (shared)
Artifacts referenced: benchmark spreadsheet (sheet link), security packet v1 (link), onboarding checklist (link)

Summary: quick check-in on onboarding gates, practical perf results from first smoke tests, discussion of access model (VPC vs hosted), and next steps to run meaningful perf sweep and finalize security scoping.
Topics: onboarding progress; creds + SSO; networking/VPC details; benchmark anomalies (tail latency); kv cache behavior; next steps and owners.

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: All right thanks everyone for joining, uh this is our weekly operability checkpoint, we've got a few things to go through — onboarding status, the smoke bench results Priya ran yesterday and some security questions. Luis is on the line too for any CS-ish items. 
[00:22] Evan: Thanks, Jordan. Quick heads up, we're on kinda a tight timeline — leadership wants an initial integration demo by Oct 26, so the pilot needs to be stable enough for that.
[00:34] Jordan: Yep noted. That's about three weeks from now, so we should prioritize access and a reproducible benchmark. Priya, you ran the smoke runs?
[00:44] Priya: Yeah I ran three passes last night against the staging cluster we provisioned. I used redwood-13b-q4 and the single-GPU dedicated pool config we discussed, cold start to warm -- and some of the numbers looked fine but there were a couple of tail spikes. I put the raw CSV in the spreadsheet. Quick high-level: median latency ~120ms per token for our 1k token synthetic, p95 ~380ms, p99 hitting 720ms on one pass.
[01:12] Marisa: That p99 is concerning for us, our SLA needs <500ms tail ideally for the demo. Were those outliers due to autoscaler or network hiccups?
[01:23] Priya: From logs the serving side showed a KV cache miss and a small GC-ish pause in the allocator; also the customer-side client had a single retry which added overhead. There might be a config mismatch — batching window was 15ms instead of 8ms, which increased latency for single-request flows.
[01:44] Evan: Can you explain the KV cache miss bit? We've got a lot of prefix reuse in our prompt patterns so cache behavior is actually pretty important.
[01:50] Priya: Sure. So Redwood runtime keeps a KV cache across steps; the bench pattern mixed short and long sequences which caused cache eviction when we were testing aggressive concurrency. That caused re-computation. We can tweak cache retention and also tune the batcher to prefer prefix coalescing to keep latency down.
[02:10] Samir: On security — we still haven't got the SSO metadata or the KMS requirements. Is the hosted mode going to store customer keys? We need clear answers for our compliance folks.
[02:22] Jordan: Good question. For hosted API we do not store customer plaintext keys; we support customer-managed keys via KMS integrations. For Private (VPC/on-prem) you can bring your own KMS/HSM. I'll send the security packet that details the flows.
[02:38] Samir: Will that include network flow diagrams and audit log retention?
[02:41] Jordan: Yes, it's in the packet. We'll add a short excerpt calling out retention windows and where audit logs are accessible.
[02:48] Ava: For the demo we also need SSO — SAML with Okta ideally. Are we ready to accept their metadata?
[02:55] Luis: We support SAML/SSO and can accept metadata; there's a small onboarding form for SSO metadata. Jordan will send it after the call.
[03:06] Jordan: I'll send the SSO metadata form and the SOC2 excerpt after.
[03:10] Evan: Back to perf — can we get a consistent benchmark with a fixed concurrency and fixed prompt set? Last night you used a synthetic long token test which may not reflect our real traffic.
[03:21] Priya: Agree. Next run we'll use their prompt set (Evan's provided sample) and run 200 concurrent clients with 2k tokens total to simulate chat history. That should reveal realistic throughput and token cost.
[03:34] Marisa: Also please run with both hosted and dedicated configs — our infra team wants to compare latency difference when routing via our VPC egress vs Redwood hosted route.
[03:42] Priya: Yep we can run side-by-side: hosted endpoint from a local client and dedicated VPC tunnel. We'll capture network RTT and server-side times.
[03:50] Evan: Any idea on cost differential? We need a ballpark to present to finance.
[03:55] Jordan: We'll include a cost delta in the bench spreadsheet — token cost estimates with and without quantization. For rough numbers, quantized 13b runs are about 40–60% cheaper than fp16 variants depending on throughput. We'll give a modeled sheet.
[04:12] Samir: A question on data residency — is there an option to pin region for logs and model execution?
[04:17] Jordan: Yes, you can pin region in Redwood Dedicated and Private; hosted also supports region selection for control plane and model execution. We can document what we've got for EU vs US.
[04:25] Ava: From a timeline POV, if we get SSO and CIDR ranges this week, can you provision the VPC tunnel by next Tuesday?
[04:33] Marisa: That depends on our firewall team but yes we can try to turn it around.
[04:36] Jordan: Assuming we get metadata by Oct 12 we'll schedule provisioning — Priya, can you coordinate?
[04:42] Priya: Yep I'll coordinate with Marisa for the tunnel and firewall rules.
[04:46] [05:00] Luis: Quick note on evals — it's useful for the pilot to have a small regression harness (50 golden prompts) to detect quality regressions across model variants. We can supply Redwood with the set and request automatic quality rollups.
[05:12] Evan: Yes we have a seed set. We want to ensure that any quantization or kernel changes don't degrade fidelity on our prompts.
[05:19] Priya: We'll run quality metrics (exact match / embedding cosine where applicable) alongside perf. That will be part of the bench artifact.
[05:27] Samir: Back to encryption — for Private we might require HSM-backed keys. Do you have a list of supported HSMs?
[05:35] Jordan: We support major cloud KMS providers and can integrate with AWS CloudHSM in Private deployments. The packet will list supported vendors and the integration mode. If you need FIPS attestation we'll add that as a follow-up.
[05:47] Ava: One thing: during the smoke we saw intermittent 503s from the gateway when we saturated with large batch sizes. Any immediate mitigations?
[05:55] Priya: Gateway 503s typically indicate either the batcher timed out due to backpressure or the model runtime OOM'd. We saw a log indicating transient OOM on a single worker; scaling up worker slice size or reducing batch max tokens resolves it. We'll test with smaller batch windows for your pattern.
[06:14] Marisa: Can you also provide guidance on autoscaler thresholds? We don't want sudden scale events during the demo.
[06:19] Priya: We'll recommend conservative CPU/GPU load thresholds and a warm pool. For demo traffic, keep guaranteed min replicas to avoid cold starts. We'll add recommended autoscale config to the checklist.
[06:31] Evan: About routing — is there a way to prefer cheaper model variants when load is high, but fall back to higher-quality variant? We'd like graceful degradation.
[06:40] Jordan: Yes that's built-in via routing policies: you can specify cost-tiered fallbacks. It can be region-aware and capacity-aware. We'll show you example routing YAML in the repo.
[06:49] Ava: Any blockers on our side other than SSO metadata and CIDRs?
[06:54] Marisa: We've got a small request: we'd like read-only audit logs pushed into our Splunk instance. Is that supported?
[07:01] Jordan: Yes, log forwarding via syslog or secure S3 export is supported. We'll include Splunk forwarding instructions in the packet.
[07:08] Samir: We also need an LA (legal) redline window — procurement expects an SLA appendix. Do you have a template?
[07:15] Luis: We have an enterprise SLA template; Jordan will connect your procurement contact with our legal ops.
[07:21] Evan: Ok returning to perf — can you explain the plan for reducing the p99 spikes?
[07:26] Priya: Actionable items: 1) tighten batcher window from 15ms to 8ms for single-request workflows, 2) increase KV cache retention and prefer prefix coalescing in the batching policy, 3) warm pool of one extra replica to remove cold start jitter. We'll run the bench with these toggles and compare.
[07:44] Marisa: That sounds good. Document the config changes so we can reproduce.
[07:47] Priya: Absolutely, every run will have an attached config diff.
[07:50] Ava: For the demo on Oct 26 — who's presenting? We want someone from Redwood on the call to help if things go sideways.
[07:58] Jordan: Jordan and Priya can present and troubleshoot live. Luis can handle procurement questions. We'll plan a dry-run with your team on Oct 23.
[08:07] Evan: Dry-run on Oct 23 works. Make sure the staging environment mirrors demo env (VPC + SSO + pre-warmed pool).
[08:17] Jordan: Noted. We'll attempt to lock the staging topology and share the exact runbook.
[08:21] Marisa: We'll open firewall windows and test the tunnel prior to the dry-run.
[08:25] [08:30] Priya: Quick clarification — for the bench we'll instrument both client-side RTT and server-side token timing so we can attribute delays. Also we'll capture GPU utilization and any GC logs.
[08:41] Samir: Please include audit log timestamps so security can correlate.
[08:44] Priya: Will do.
[08:46] Jordan: Any other open items?
[08:49] Evan: One more — can Redwood support masked tokens / redaction at inference time? We need to ensure PHI is not logged in cleartext to any external storage.
[09:00] Jordan: We support token-level redaction and you can disable control plane logging of prompts; for hosted we provide options to strip PII before persisting. We'll include redaction guidance in the packet.
[09:12] Samir: Good. That addresses a lot.
[09:15] Ava: OK recap?
[09:17] Jordan: Recap: 1) Jordan to send security packet + SSO form by Oct 9; 2) Priya to run the targeted bench (customer prompt set, 200 concurrency, compare hosted vs VPC) and upload CSV by Oct 14; 3) Marisa to provide CIDRs and firewall windows by Oct 12; 4) Schedule dry-run Oct 23 and demo Oct 26. Action owners captured in sheet.
[09:45] Evan: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[09:48] Jordan: Thanks, we'll follow up in email with links.
[09:52] Meeting ends.

title: Granite Harbor - Private Deployment & Connectivity Intake
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-10-06 16:00 PDT
Duration: 54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Marcus (Redwood NetEng), Alyssa (GH Head of Cloud), Diego (GH Security), Nora (GH Eng Mgr), Samir (GH SRE)

Auto-summary (auto):
- Customer evaluating private deployment (VPC peering vs PrivateLink) for PII workloads; primary concerns: egress control, regional isolation, and KMS integration.
- Next step: network workshop and pilot pricing/SOW.

00:00 Jordan: Hey everyone thanks for carving out time. quick intros?
00:08 Alyssa: Alyssa here, head of cloud platforms at Granite Harbor. our main ask is we need to run models against customer data without leaving our VPCs. compliance is big.
00:16 Jordan: Totally, that's the common ask. Priya and Marcus from our side will cover networking details and security posture.
00:22 Priya: hi all — I'll start with a high-level of what Redwood Private looks like and then we can sketch connectivity patterns.
00:30 Priya: So with Private you get a control plane we host but network egress into your VPC is via Private Endpoints or VPC Peering depending on cloud. per-customer isolation, optional transit gateway integration, and you can pin models and rollout rules.
00:45 Diego: quick clarifying q — by "Private Endpoint" do you mean cloud-private link-style endpoints where traffic never traverses public internet? we require that.
00:53 Priya: yes. that's the idea. for AWS we support Interface VPC endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) and also VPC peering / transit gateway options. for GCP we use VPC peering + private service connect patterns.
01:06 Samir: what's the typical egress path? our compliance folks want to ensure all egress hits our firewall for DLP.
01:13 Marcus: good question. we generally recommend two patterns: 1) private endpoint (interface ENI in your subnet) so traffic is inside your VPC and routed locally; or 2) VPC peering/transit gateway where you peer to Redwood-owned VPCs and route through your egress appliances. either can be forced through your firewall via route tables or TGW attachments.
01:34 Nora: and IP addressing — will we need to allocate /26 per environment or how does that planning look?
01:39 Priya: typically PrivateLink uses ENIs and you need a small pool of free IPs on the subnets where endpoints will be created. for peering we recommend planning for outbound NAT ranges we will document. we'll send guidance but usually a /28 — /24 depending on scale.
01:58 Diego: KMS integration — can we bring our own keys? all tokens and logs need to be encrypted with customer keys.
02:05 Marcus: We support customer-managed keys for data at rest on Private deployments. the control plane communicates over mTLS and we can integrate with your KMS via cross-account roles/CMKs for AWS or KMS keys in other clouds. we'll need an IAM role to grant decryption access to the Redwood infra.
02:24 Alyssa: is there any customer data egress back to Redwood control plane in plaintext? our security policy is no plaintext PII leaving region.
02:32 Priya: No plaintext. model inference occurs in the private dataplane. logs and metrics can be forwarded to the control plane but we can redact or stripe PII; additionally we can store logs in your s3 via log forwarder if you prefer. we have a SOC2 attestation and can provide the security pack.
02:49 Diego: good — we'll want the security pack. also question about audit logs and SAML SSO provisioning for RBAC.
02:55 Jordan: we have SAML & SCIM for provisioning, RBAC with org roles, and audit logs for admin actions. we'll attach the compliance docs after the call.
03:05 Alyssa: region constraints — some of our customers are EU-only (data residency). can we pin Redwood compute to eu-west-1 only?
03:13 Priya: yes, you can choose region placement for the dataplane. for Dedicated/Private we can provision in a single region or multi-region as needed. there are cost and latency implications for multi-region; we'll highlight that in the design workshop.
03:27 Nora: for latency sensitive queries we might want a near-region cache. do you support cross-region KV cache sync?
03:34 Marcus: we have a couple of options: colocated regional deployments with async KV replication or routing rules to send latency-critical requests to the nearest regional instance. caching coherence is eventual; we'll discuss tradeoffs in the workshop.
03:46 Diego: firewall rules: do you publish maintained IP blocks we can allow-list?
03:51 Priya: for peering it's all in your VPC so no published IPs to allow, but for PrivateLink we will create ENIs in your subnets so nothing to allow outbound — if you use NAT restrictions you just need to allow the ENI. for cases where you must restrict specific egress to our control plane, we publish a small set of control plane egress ranges and we'll provide a maintained list via a URL.
04:14 Samir: transit gateway story — we have a central TGW and prefer all third-party connectivity through it. can Redwood peer to our TGW?
04:20 Marcus: yes. we can peer into your TGW via transit VPC peering or by establishing peering to a Redwood TGW and then propagating routes. we'll need to align on route propagation and whether you want route tables owning the default route to force traffic through your egress appliances.
04:35 Alyssa: what about private DNS? we run split-horizon DNS. will the endpoints resolve correctly?
04:40 Priya: for PrivateLink we create interface endpoints and you can control the private DNS entries. for peering scenario we typically provide private hostnames and you can map them in your private hosted zones. we'll document the steps for Route53/R53 equivalents.
04:55 Diego: are there any ports/traffic patterns we should pre-approve?
05:00 Marcus: mostly TLS on 443 between your services and the endpoint. control-plane websocket traffic for streaming uses 443 as well. if you use mTLS there will be mutual cert validation. also for KV cache sync we sometimes use long-lived connections over 443.
05:18 Nora: what's the onboarding timeline if we start a pilot next week?
05:23 Jordan: pilot typical timeline is 2-4 weeks: week 1 network and security workshop + VPC config; week 2 provisioning and smoke test; week 3 integration and performance run; week 4 test validations and handoff.
05:36 Alyssa: any blockers you've seen with banks/financials we should be aware of?
05:40 Priya: primary blockers: strict egress gating (shops that want all traffic to go through a specific proxy), KMS key trust boundaries, and IP allocation when subnets are tight. also legal often asks for additional SOC2 / encryption detail — we can provide that.
05:56 Diego: we will want to run an internal pen test scenario — do you support customer-initiated pen tests against the private dataplane?
06:03 Marcus: yes, on Private deployments we support customer-initiated tests on a schedule with agreed scope. we require notice and an approved test plan. we'll share the policy.
06:15 Alyssa: how do upgrades work — if Redwood upgrades runtime or kernels does that trigger downtime for us?
06:21 Priya: for Private we offer maintenance windows and can do in-place updates with rolling upgrades. we can also schedule canary upgrades to a subset of nodes and failover. no forced downtime for typical patching.
06:33 Samir: curious about monitoring — can we ship metrics to our prometheus and logs to our ELK?
06:37 Marcus: yes, we can push metrics to your Prometheus remote write or provide a scrape endpoint inside your VPC. logs can be forwarded to your S3/ELK via agents.
06:46 Jordan: quick commercial question — are you evaluating Redwood hosted as a fallback or is Private the only acceptable route?
06:52 Alyssa: Private first. we can consider hosted for low-risk non-PII workloads but our initial pilot needs to be fully private.
06:59 Jordan: understood. last bit — who are the stakeholders for the pilot approval?
07:03 Alyssa: security (Diego), infra (me + Samir), legal for contracts, and product owners for success metrics.
07:10 Priya: ok we'll propose a workshop date — next steps we'll send you the network checklist and a proposed SOW + pricing.
07:16 Diego: any pre-reqs from our side for the workshop?
07:20 Marcus: please have the VPC CIDRs, a diagram of TGW/route tables, and the firewall egress rules. if you have an IAM admin to review cross-account roles that will help.
07:33 Jordan: thanks everyone, we'll follow up with the docs and schedule the workshop.
07:38 Alyssa: thanks — appreciate the level of detail.

End of transcript.

References shared in chat: https://redwood.example.com/private-vpc-checklist (doc), https://redwood.example.com/security-pack (security pack), pricing spreadsheet (sent after call).

title: Neptune Analytics - CUDA RTC lock + KV-cache stall deep dive
preview: Deep technical review of recurring CUDA RTC lockups causing GPU stalls and KV-cache saturation during mixed seq throughput. Discussed root-cause hypotheses (driver preemption, async kernel scheduling, prefetch throttling), short-term mitigations (aggressive kvcache backoff, request sharding, smaller batch windows), and long-term fixes (runtime watchdog, kernel selector tweaks, driver knob matrix). Agreed next steps: Redwood to run instrumented repro with tuned driver/RT settings, share trace collection script, Neptune to provide small repro trace and priority logs, align on pilot test window.

title: Sprint 4: Latency & Throughput Gap Review + Fallback Playbook — AstridCorp POC
preview: Week 4 readout: Redwood matches median latency vs incumbent on short, variable prompts but shows higher tail (p95/p99) under burst and cold-cache conditions. Throughput (tokens/sec) is comparable when batching is tuned; core gap appears in cache warmup and routing under multi-tenant noise. Agreed playbook: run steady-state warm-cache head-to-head, enable prefix caching, tweak batch window for medium prompts, and schedule dedicated-slot run. Security docs and routing workbook to be shared.

title: Nova Sentinel - Data handling & training assurance
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-08-21
Start: 15:00 PDT
Duration: ~47 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Shah (Redwood SE), Daniel Kim (Nova CISO), Marta Alvarez (Nova MLOps), Liam O'Connor (Nova Product)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
- Nova Sentinel wants written assurance that customer data will not be used to train models and needs details around KMS/HSM, per-tenant keys, audit log delivery, and retention windows for prompts.
- Redwood reps explained default no-training policy, options for ephemeral prompts, VPC/private deployment choices, and KMS/HSM integration patterns.
- Agreed next steps: SOC2 docs, test VPC + PHI redaction benchmark, and contract language draft.

Topics (rough):
- No-train on customer data confirmation
- Prompt retention / ephemeral prompts
- KMS / HSM and key rotation
- Audit logs and SIEM integration (CloudWatch/Splunk/S3)
- RBAC / SSO / SCIM provisioning
- Data residency and private deployment options

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan (Redwood): Hey everyone, thanks for joining, appreciate you making time. quick roll call?
[00:10] Daniel (Nova): Daniel here, Marta, Liam on my side. thanks.
[00:15] Marta (Nova): hi.
[00:20] Jordan: Cool. This call is mostly to run through the security posture and specifically the data handling questions you sent over, so we can make sure legal and security are aligned. Priya, want to intro yourself?
[00:28] Priya (Redwood-SE): Yeah hi all, Priya Shah, I'm the SE on the infra side. I'll cover technical details around KMS, VPC, and prompt retention flows.
[00:35] Liam (Nova): quick note, we have two priorities: 1) we cannot have PHI used to improve models; 2) we need strong audit logs for every inference and for key access.
[00:44] Priya: totally understood. to start high level, Redwood's hosted API has a default operational stance where customer content is not used to train upstream models, and we can provide a signed attestation. For regulated workloads we recommend Redwood Private or Dedicated which gives options for per-tenant encryption and tighter retention controls.
[01:05] Daniel: can you confirm "not used to train" in writing? we need that in contract and as a security attestation for our auditors.
[01:12] Jordan: yes, we can share a formal attestation. I'll attach SOC2 Type II and the attestation PDF after the call. link we'll use: https://redwood.com/security/soc2-2026.pdf (note - placeholder link shared during call).
[01:26] Marta: ok. second, keys: do you support customer-managed keys that rotate? HSM? we need key rotation and split-knowledge for some functions.
[01:34] Priya: we support KMS integrations for both cloud KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS) and customer-managed HSMs via external key managers. We also have an envelope encryption layer so you can bring your own CEK with a CMK in KMS. Key rotation is supported, and we log key operations. See docs: https://redwood.com/docs/kms-integration
[01:55] Liam: how fine-grained is the logging? we want a log entry for each inference that includes a key-id, model-id, route, and a hash of the prompt, not the prompt content.
[02:05] Priya: current pattern: audit logs capture request metadata, model id, route, tokens consumed, timestamps, and can optionally include a non-reversible prompt fingerprint. We advise not storing raw prompts in long-term storage for PHI. You can stream logs to your S3 or Splunk for retention. Example config is in the security deck I'll share.
[02:30] Daniel: ok that's good, do you have a sample logging schema?
[02:34] Jordan: yes will attach a minimal schema in the follow-up.
[02:38] Marta: retention windows for prompts — what's the default and can we set to zero for ephemeral? we need a workflow where prompts are ephemeral for PII/PHI.
[02:47] Priya: default for hosted is 30 days for telemetry and 0 for model training usage (again we don't train on hosted customer data). For Dedicated and Private you can configure retention down to zero or disable persistence entirely. There is a prompt-ephemerality flag (internally sometimes called the "prompt ephemera flag" — sorry, that's the internal name), which bypasses persistent logging of prompt content and only keeps the fingerprint for a configurable TTL.
[03:10] Liam: the "ephemera" name might be a problem for legal, haha.
[03:14] Jordan: yeah we can surface the feature as "ephemeral prompt mode" in the contract language.
[03:18] Daniel: about the "no-train" — does that apply across all model variants, or only to the model you pin? e.g., if there's fallback to a cheaper variant during outage, could that cheaper model ingest examples?
[03:30] Priya: good question. training opt-out is at the tenant level and propagates across model variants in production. fallback behavior is controlled by routing policy; we have a policy construct that ensures the same training opt-out flag follows requests. If you opt-out, none of the models will be added to training datasets. We can also lock routing to only certain verified model families.
[03:54] Marta: do you do any aggregation or anonymization for telemetry that could leak signal? our auditors ask about dataset leakage from aggregated logs.
[04:02] Priya: telemetry aggregates like latency percentiles and token counts are computed without including content. Aggregated metrics are not used for model training. we also have a clean-room analytics pipeline if you want to run deeper queries on telemetry without exposing content.
[04:22] Liam: can we get a signed "no training" clause? It can't be an opt-out buried in the terms.
[04:28] Jordan: yep, we'll provide a signed attestation and an explicit contract clause that can be added. next step listed.
[04:33] Daniel: on RBAC and SSO — does Redwood support SCIM and SAML? we need group mappings and admin separation.
[04:40] Priya: yes SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, and role-based access controls. admins can define roles at org and project level, with audit trails for role changes. we can demonstrate SCIM provisioning in the POC.
[04:58] Liam: does RBAC extend to the logs? e.g., only security team can read full request logs but product team gets aggregated metrics.
[05:04] Priya: yep, audit logs are configurable with ACLs. You can stream raw logs to a customer-owned S3 with bucket policies to control who reads them.
[05:14] Marta: on data residency — we operate in eu-west and need european data residency. can logs and keys stay in region?
[05:22] Jordan: for Redwood Dedicated/Private we can guarantee region residency, and for hosted you can pick region endpoints — logs and keys can be configured to stay within eu-west for EU customers. for keys in KMS you can select a regional CMK.
[05:36] Daniel: what about private deployments — we were thinking about an air-gapped option for some workflows.
[05:40] Priya: we offer an on-prem package for air-gapped installs; that includes local KMS integration and local audit endpoints. note that the private control plane still needs limited connectivity for control signals unless you go full air-gap mode which is a heavier deployment.
[06:00] Unknown Speaker: "kay-vee cache" question?
[06:03] Priya: ha yes sorry — we say KV cache, key-value cache for autoregressive state. the cache never persists to long-term storage unless you opt in; it's ephemeral per inference.
[06:15] Liam: do you support tokenization and PHI redaction before sending data to model? we want a pre-send hook for redaction.
[06:22] Priya: yes, we have pre-request plugins you can run in the VPC that will do redaction or tokenization. for hosted we also offer a hosted pre-processing layer but for PHI we recommend running pre-send hooks in your VPC so raw content never leaves your network.
[06:38] Marta: good. examples of legal language for the data handling clause?
[06:42] Jordan: we'll share a sample clause. main points: no-training attestation, ephemeral prompt mode option, KMS-holdings, data residency commitment, audit log delivery.
[06:54] Daniel: logging retention... you said default 30 days. we need a 0-day retention for prompts and 90-day for metadata only, are you able to set that?
[07:03] Priya: yes for Dedicated/Private you can set content retention to zero and keep metadata for 90 days. For hosted we can do a custom retention config under an enterprise agreement.
[07:16] Liam: can you provide a diagram of the end-to-end flow showing where the prompt is ephemeral vs where the fingerprint is stored, and how KMS wraps CEKs?
[07:24] Priya: yes I'll attach a flow diagram in the follow-up; it's in our security deck.
[07:29] Jordan: speaking of attachments, I'll drop the SOC2 link and the logging schema and retention spreadsheet after this call: https://drive.redwood/team/nova-sentinel/prompt-retention.xlsx
[07:38] Marta: awesome. on the training attestation — how is that enforced? is it manual or automated within your pipeline?
[07:45] Priya: the enforcement is automated: we have tenant flags and a data store that marks requests as not-to-train, and any candidate examples routed to training pipelines are filtered by that flag. there is also an audit gate where sampled examples are checked against the tenant opt-outs.
[08:02] Daniel: do you have a third-party attestation for the filtering logic? we need controls that the auditors can test.
[08:09] Jordan: we can include the filtering logic description in the SOC2 artifact and add test cases for auditors; we also include change logs for the filter rules in our audit trail.
[08:21] Liam: how about backups of logs — if logs are backed up to S3, do backups respect the TTL deletion?
[08:28] Priya: yes lifecycle policies are applied so backups are subject to the same retention TTLs. If you control the S3 you set the lifecycle rules; for hosted we can configure lifecycle on our buckets per your policy.
[08:42] Marta: what about data at rest encryption and HSM-backed keys for model parameters?
[08:48] Priya: model weights are on encrypted volumes; customer data in transit is TLS, at rest encrypted with CMKs. HSM integration is available for key wrapping and to meet FIPS/HSM requirements.
[09:00] Daniel: we may want to demo the full flow with our sample set. what's the POC timeline you recommend?
[09:06] Jordan: typically 2-4 weeks for a discovery + benchmark: week 1 provisioning and baseline, week 2 run PHI redaction and latency/cost benchmarks, then review. Priya will provision the test VPC.
[09:16] Marta: good. can we also test failover routing? we want to see the behavior when capacity is constrained and ensure training opt-out persists.
[09:24] Priya: yes we'll set a routing policy that pins fallback models and preserves the tenant-level opt-out.
[09:30] Liam: a few nitty things — can we rotate the CMKs without downtime?
[09:34] Priya: yes we support key rotation with automatic rewrap of CEKs, typically seamless; we'll show the rotation playbook.
[09:42] Daniel: auditors will want change control evidence for KMS policy changes. do you keep an audit trail for IAM/KMS policy edits?
[09:50] Priya: yes all admin actions around KMS and IAM are logged and can be exported to your SIEM.
[09:57] Jordan: quick question from our side — do you need a signed "no train" clause for all environments including dev/staging?

- Redwood reps explained default no-training policy, options for ephemeral prompts, VPC/private deployment choices, and KMS/HSM integration patterns.
- Agreed next steps: SOC2 docs, test VPC + PHI redaction benchmark, and contract language draft.

Topics (rough):
- No-train on customer data confirmation
- Prompt retention / ephemeral prompts

[00:00] Jordan (Redwood): Hey everyone, thanks for joining, appreciate you making time. quick roll call?
[00:10] Daniel (Nova): Daniel here, Marta, Liam on my side. thanks.
[00:15] Marta (Nova): hi.
[00:20] Jordan: Cool. This call is mostly to run through the security posture and specifically the data handling questions you sent over, so we can make sure legal and security are aligned. Priya, want to intro yourself?
[00:28] Priya (Redwood-SE): Yeah hi all, Priya Shah, I'm the SE on the infra side. I'll cover technical details around KMS, VPC, and prompt retention flows.
[00:35] Liam (Nova): quick note, we have two priorities: 1) we cannot have PHI used to improve models; 2) we need strong audit logs for every inference and for key access.
[00:44] Priya: totally understood. to start high level, Redwood's hosted API has a default operational stance where customer content is not used to train upstream models, and we can provide a signed attestation. For regulated workloads we recommend Redwood Private or Dedicated which gives options for per-tenant encryption and tighter retention controls.
[01:05] Daniel: can you confirm "not used to train" in writing? we need that in contract and as a security attestation for our auditors.
[01:12] Jordan: yes, we can share a formal attestation. I'll attach SOC2 Type II and the attestation PDF after the call. link we'll use: https://redwood.com/security/soc2-2026.pdf (note - placeholder link shared during call).
[01:26] Marta: ok. second, keys: do you support customer-managed keys that rotate? HSM? we need key rotation and split-knowledge for some functions.
[01:34] Priya: we support KMS integrations for both cloud KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS) and customer-managed HSMs via external key managers. We also have an envelope encryption layer so you can bring your own CEK with a CMK in KMS. Key rotation is supported, and we log key operations. See docs: https://redwood.com/docs/kms-integration
[01:55] Liam: how fine-grained is the logging? we want a log entry for each inference that includes a key-id, model-id, route, and a hash of the prompt, not the prompt content.
[02:05] Priya: current pattern: audit logs capture request metadata, model id, route, tokens consumed, timestamps, and can optionally include a non-reversible prompt fingerprint. We advise not storing raw prompts in long-term storage for PHI. You can stream logs to your S3 or Splunk for retention. Example config is in the security deck I'll share.
[02:30] Daniel: ok that's good, do you have a sample logging schema?
[02:34] Jordan: yes will attach a minimal schema in the follow-up.
[02:38] Marta: retention windows for prompts — what's the default and can we set to zero for ephemeral? we need a workflow where prompts are ephemeral for PII/PHI.

[03:14] Jordan: yeah we can surface the feature as "ephemeral prompt mode" in the contract language.

[03:30] Priya: good question. training opt-out is at the tenant level and propagates across model variants in production. fallback behavior is controlled by routing policy; we have a policy construct that ensures the same training opt-out flag follows requests. If you opt-out, none of the models will be added to training datasets. We can also lock routing to only certain verified model families.
[03:54] Marta: do you do any aggregation or anonymization for telemetry that could leak signal? our auditors ask about dataset leakage from aggregated logs.
[04:02] Priya: telemetry aggregates like latency percentiles and token counts are computed without including content. Aggregated metrics are not used for model training. we also have a clean-room analytics pipeline if you want to run deeper queries on telemetry without exposing content.
[04:22] Liam: can we get a signed "no training" clause? It can't be an opt-out buried in the terms.
[04:28] Jordan: yep, we'll provide a signed attestation and an explicit contract clause that can be added. next step listed.
[04:33] Daniel: on RBAC and SSO — does Redwood support SCIM and SAML? we need group mappings and admin separation.
[04:40] Priya: yes SAML SSO, SCIM provisioning, and role-based access controls. admins can define roles at org and project level, with audit trails for role changes. we can demonstrate SCIM provisioning in the POC.
[04:58] Liam: does RBAC extend to the logs? e.g., only security team can read full request logs but product team gets aggregated metrics.
[05:04] Priya: yep, audit logs are configurable with ACLs. You can stream raw logs to a customer-owned S3 with bucket policies to control who reads them.
[05:14] Marta: on data residency — we operate in eu-west and need european data residency. can logs and keys stay in region?
[05:22] Jordan: for Redwood Dedicated/Private we can guarantee region residency, and for hosted you can pick region endpoints — logs and keys can be configured to stay within eu-west for EU customers. for keys in KMS you can select a regional CMK.
[05:36] Daniel: what about private deployments — we were thinking about an air-gapped option for some workflows.
[05:40] Priya: we offer an on-prem package for air-gapped installs; that includes local KMS integration and local audit endpoints. note that the private control plane still needs limited connectivity for control signals unless you go full air-gap mode which is a heavier deployment.
[06:00] Unknown Speaker: "kay-vee cache" question?
[06:03] Priya: ha yes sorry — we say KV cache, key-value cache for autoregressive state. the cache never persists to long-term storage unless you opt in; it's ephemeral per inference.
[06:15] Liam: do you support tokenization and PHI redaction before sending data to model? we want a pre-send hook for redaction.
[06:22] Priya: yes, we have pre-request plugins you can run in the VPC that will do redaction or tokenization. for hosted we also offer a hosted pre-processing layer but for PHI we recommend running pre-send hooks in your VPC so raw content never leaves your network.
[06:38] Marta: good. examples of legal language for the data handling clause?
[06:42] Jordan: we'll share a sample clause. main points: no-training attestation, ephemeral prompt mode option, KMS-holdings, data residency commitment, audit log delivery.
[06:54] Daniel: logging retention... you said default 30 days. we need a 0-day retention for prompts and 90-day for metadata only, are you able to set that?
[07:03] Priya: yes for Dedicated/Private you can set content retention to zero and keep metadata for 90 days. For hosted we can do a custom retention config under an enterprise agreement.
[07:16] Liam: can you provide a diagram of the end-to-end flow showing where the prompt is ephemeral vs where the fingerprint is stored, and how KMS wraps CEKs?
[07:24] Priya: yes I'll attach a flow diagram in the follow-up; it's in our security deck.
[07:29] Jordan: speaking of attachments, I'll drop the SOC2 link and the logging schema and retention spreadsheet after this call: https://drive.redwood/team/nova-sentinel/prompt-retention.xlsx
[07:38] Marta: awesome. on the training attestation — how is that enforced? is it manual or automated within your pipeline?
[07:45] Priya: the enforcement is automated: we have tenant flags and a data store that marks requests as not-to-train, and any candidate examples routed to training pipelines are filtered by that flag. there is also an audit gate where sampled examples are checked against the tenant opt-outs.
[08:02] Daniel: do you have a third-party attestation for the filtering logic? we need controls that the auditors can test.
[08:09] Jordan: we can include the filtering logic description in the SOC2 artifact and add test cases for auditors; we also include change logs for the filter rules in our audit trail.
[08:21] Liam: how about backups of logs — if logs are backed up to S3, do backups respect the TTL deletion?
[08:28] Priya: yes lifecycle policies are applied so backups are subject to the same retention TTLs. If you control the S3 you set the lifecycle rules; for hosted we can configure lifecycle on our buckets per your policy.
[08:42] Marta: what about data at rest encryption and HSM-backed keys for model parameters?
[08:48] Priya: model weights are on encrypted volumes; customer data in transit is TLS, at rest encrypted with CMKs. HSM integration is available for key wrapping and to meet FIPS/HSM requirements.
[09:00] Daniel: we may want to demo the full flow with our sample set. what's the POC timeline you recommend?
[09:06] Jordan: typically 2-4 weeks for a discovery + benchmark: week 1 provisioning and baseline, week 2 run PHI redaction and latency/cost benchmarks, then review. Priya will provision the test VPC.
[09:16] Marta: good. can we also test failover routing? we want to see the behavior when capacity is constrained and ensure training opt-out persists.
[09:24] Priya: yes we'll set a routing policy that pins fallback models and preserves the tenant-level opt-out.
[09:30] Liam: a few nitty things — can we rotate the CMKs without downtime?
[09:34] Priya: yes we support key rotation with automatic rewrap of CEKs, typically seamless; we'll show the rotation playbook.
[09:42] Daniel: auditors will want change control evidence for KMS policy changes. do you keep an audit trail for IAM/KMS policy edits?

title: Zephyr Analytics - Activation Handoff & Runtime Intake

Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2026-07-20 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~47 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Priya Sharma (Redwood SE), Lina Torres (Zephyr), Marcus Lee (Zephyr), Derek Nguyen (Zephyr)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be fuzzy):
- Kickoff/handoff from sales to success/ops; focus on runtime sizing, VPC/private deployment questions, security artifacts and timeline for pilot.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan (Redwood AE): hey everyone thanks for joining, we're gonna just do like a quick activation handoff and runtime intake, i've got priya here from the eng team, uh she's the se who's gonna drive the sizing and runtime notes.
[00:17] Priya (Redwood SE): hey all, hi lina, marcus, derek, thanks for carving out time. quick ask: if you can have your traffic profile ready — qps, typical sequence lengths — that'll help.
[00:30] Lina (Zephyr): yeah yeah, thanks. we tried to put that into the intake form but it's pretty rough, we have a couple of product flows, primary is customer support summarization which is low qps but strict latency, and a batch embed job for search that can tolerate higher latency.
[00:45] Marcus (Zephyr): to add, timeline is kinda urgent for us, pilot needs to be ready in three weeks, that's internal timeline from the product team.
[00:55] Jordan: noted. three weeks is tight but doable for a pilot — depends on network work and whether you go hosted or dedicated. can we talk about their tradeoffs now?
[01:10] Derek (Zephyr): sure, we were leaning to Redwood Dedicated because of predictable throughput and data separation, but curious about cost delta vs hosted. Also need to confirm we can use our KMS and existing SSO.
[01:25] Priya: Dedicated will give you reserved GPU pools — predictable p99s — but there's a ramp and we need infra details for peering. Hosted is faster to get started but you'd be on shared infra; data controls are stronger with dedicated or Private/VPC. small note, sometimes the transcript has product names off — we run as "Redwood Dedica" in older notes lol.
[01:45] Marcus: haha that's fine. security is non-negotiable for us — SOC 2 and audit logs; we need to know retention windows and whether logs can stream to our SIEM.
[02:02] Jordan: yes, we support exporting audit logs and can map retention windows, i'll attach the SOC2 and security pack after. priya, can you summarize where we'd need derek's team to jump in?
[02:15] Priya: sure — top three items: 1) VPC peering or private link details (CIDR, firewall rules), 2) IAM contacts + SAML metadata for SSO, 3) any egress rules for third-party analytics. plus a small list of test credentials.
[02:34] Derek: we can get the CIDRs and an outboard infrastructure engineer to share that by friday, 2026-07-23.
[02:42] Jordan: perfect. ok let's pivot to runtime sizing — priya?
[02:49] Priya: based on what you told us: two primary workloads. For the support summarization: p95 latency goal <500ms, qps ~2-5 at peak. For the embedding batch job: qps bursts of up to 50 but can be batched overnight. We'll propose a baseline of 2 small GPU nodes with autoscale for bursts, or a single dedicated medium pool depending on your concurrency. note: quantization options can reduce memory but may trade off some quality on older open models.
[03:26] Marcus: how do we evaluate that tradeoff? our quality bar is quite high for summarization.
[03:34] Priya: we run prompt evals and can do a regression harness — you give us a seed set of 50 examples, we'll run A/B against quantized and full-fp models and report rouge/metric diffs. also our Optimize tool can recommend quant profiles.
[03:50] Lina: ok we can prepare that. also how does caching work? we have recurring prefixes in support chats.
[03:58] Priya: Redwood supports prefix/KV caching — it'll help reduce token cost for repeated context. for your low-latency flows we might enable a small in-memory KV cache on the node. caching also influences routing decisions. sometimes notes will say "KV cash" in the raw transcript due to speech-to-text.
[04:18] Derek: network constraints — our infra team might require that the initial pilot stays in our VPC and we use private deployment. is that possible on a pilot timeline?
[04:29] Jordan: yes, Private/VPC is an option but it's more work upfront; Dedicated with VPC peering could be a middle ground. we'll need your network team to sign a short peering checklist.

title: Silvershore Integration — KMS/VPC & Marketplace Readiness Huddle
preview:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2026-07-02
Time: 09:00 AM EDT
Duration: 62 minutes
Location: Zoom
Recording: Enabled
Notes link: https://redwood.app/shared/silvershore-onboard

Summary:
Quick technical sync between Redwood AE/SE and Silvershore infra/security to validate readiness for marketplace listing and a short dedicated pilot. Focus areas: KMS integration, VPC/networking, packaging (Helm/OCI listing), and a light perf/quantization compatibility check.

Topics:
- Marketplace packaging and listing requirements
- KMS/HSM and key rotation expectations
- VPC peering, egress, and NAT requirements for private deployments
- Quantization compatibility and runtime kernels
- Billing meter considerations for marketplace entitlements

Action items (recorded above)

Transcript:
[00:00] Alex Kim: "Alright everybody, thanks for hopping on. uh today's, the goal is to, to get a short checkpoint on the listing readiness and the KMS/VPC questions we got after the PRD. Priya from my side will run the deep technical bits. Dana, do you want to kick off with any updates on your side?"
[00:18] Dana Ortiz: "Yeah, thanks. um we, we've locked down the internal approval to move forward with a marketplace listing, and Mark's team is standing by for the network details. Security wants to make sure KMS behavior meets our rotation policy. So, mostly just clarifying how Redwood will connect to our KMS for the private deployment option."
[00:35] Mark Liu: "And also the peering, we, we have a couple subnets that we want to avoid conflicts on, so we need the exact CIDR ranges and any requirements for NACLs. We got, like, some previous partners that needed custom NAT rules."
[00:48] Priya Nair (SE): "Cool, yeah. We can run through the VPC peering doc — the main things are: outbound to Redwood control plane on port 443, optional private link if you want no egress, and host-based routing for the GPU nodes. Also, we do a small healthcheck to specific endpoints, so we need the firewall to allow those. I'll pull up the slide in a second."
[01:12] Ollie Chen: "Quick question — on the KMS side: does Redwood require direct HSM access or do you support customer-managed KMS via a connector? Some of our compliance folks insist on HSM-backed keys for model encryption in transit and at rest."
[01:28] Jordan Blake: "Short answer: we support both. For private deployments we can integrate with customer KMS (we've done AWS KMS, GCP KMS, and also partner HSM connectors). The control plane stores only envelopes, and the key material can stay in your KMS. For marketplace listing we have a packaging option that uses a customer-provided key during install. There's some nuance around key rotation which we should align on."
[01:49] Dana Ortiz: "When you say envelope — sorry, can you dumb it down, like where are keys stored and how are they accessed at runtime?"
[01:55] Priya Nair (SE): "Yeah absolutely. So the model artifacts are encrypted at rest with a data key; that data key's encrypted with your KMS key — that's the envelope. Redwood's runtime requests a decrypt call to the KMS to unwrap the data key at startup. We never keep the plaintext key persisted. The control plane has an auth token that can be scoped to allow only decrypt requests and we log usage to the audit trail. That meets most SOC2/ISO flows."
[02:20] Ollie Chen: "Okay, that's promising. What about key rotation? Our policy rotates every 14 days. Will re-wrapping be disruptive?"
[02:27] Priya Nair (SE): "Rotation is supported but, yes, rewrap requires either a short rolling restart or an in-place rewrap mechanism. Our recommended flow for a strict 14-day turnover is automated rewrap hooks during a canary rollout window — we can schedule the rewrap to happen on the non-critical replica first. There might be a tiny blip in request latency while caches refresh, but no data loss."
[02:46] Mark Liu: "Cache — do you mean the KV cash? Sorry, did I say it wrong earlier."
[02:51] Alex Kim: "KV cache, yeah. KV cache for model key/value memory. There's also prefix caching. Both are impacted briefly when keys rotate because the runtime re-inits."
[03:03] Dana Ortiz: "Let's talk packaging — we saw the marketplace checklist briefly. Which artifacts do you need from us for the listing? Docker images? Helm chart? Any signing?"
[03:12] Alex Kim: "Good question. For Redwood marketplace we accept OCI bundles plus a Helm wrapper. Required pieces: the runtime helm chart with values.yaml that maps to customer parameters, an OCI image or images, a manifest that declares the KMS integration points, and a security scan report. Optionally, a quickstart doc and example TF module. We also sign the listing on our side and we can accept signed images from your CI."
[03:36] Priya Varma (Customer): "We currently build images and push to our ECR; we can produce a matching OCI index but we'd prefer Redwood to pull on install. Does that work?"
[03:44] Jordan Blake: "Yes — we support pulling from customer registries, we'll need a service-account with read access. For private listings we recommend including a minimal mirror in Redwood's marketplace registry for faster installs, but it's optional."
[03:58] Mark Liu: "About networking: do you need a private link endpoint or just regular peering? We have restrictions that disallow public egress from the VPC."
[04:06] Priya Nair (SE): "PrivateLink is available for enterprise customers. If you disallow public egress we can set up a VPC endpoint / private link so the control plane communicates over the private path. The install also supports an offline mode for air-gapped setups but that one needs more ops time."
[04:24] Dana Ortiz: "Perf — we want to do a small pilot on a dedicated pool to validate latency and cost. Do you have guidance on sample configs? Also, quantization — our models are Llama-variant, and we've seen mixed support for int8 quantized runtimes."
[04:39] Priya Nair (SE): "We can do a two-step: (1) a quick quant-compat test where we run a 30-minute inference pass using your model converted to a quantized format (we've seen FP16 -> int8 converters like GPTQ). We'll validate memory footprint and kernel compatibility; (2) a short perf run on a 2-GPU dedicated pool with the sequence-length distribution you expect. For Llama-variants we support multiple kernels; sometimes the int8 path needs a specific tune. We'll surface any hooks or codegen failures."
[05:04] Ollie Chen: "If we hand off a small model snapshot and a sample prompt set, do you run it on our own repo, or do you pull the artifact into Redwood test infra? Compliance-wise we need an audit trail."
[05:16] Jordan Blake: "We prefer you to give an internal test artifact or mirror it to a secured bucket. For the pilot we can run within a Redwood dedicated cluster isolated to your account; all operations get logged and we share the trace logs. We'll also provide a limited-time access role for you to inspect runtime logs."
[05:32] Dana Ortiz: "Sounds good. Billing question — if we list on the marketplace, how are entitlements handled for per-token metering vs a reserved capacity commitment? We're leaning towards a subscription + burst model."
[05:42] Alex Kim: "Marketplace supports several SKU types: on-demand per-token, reserved capacity (we've done monthly/annual committs), and hybrid entitlements where you pre-buy a baseline and overflow is metered. For listing we typically include a billing manifest that maps SKUs to meter IDs; the marketplace takes care of invoicing. We'll share the sample billing manifest so you can model costs."
[06:00] Mark Liu: "We also have to ensure telemetry doesn't leak customer PII — any outbound traces or metrics need to be redacted."
[06:07] Priya Nair (SE): "Absolutely. Telemetry fields are configurable. Our default masks payloads, and we can disable certain traces or send them to your telemetry endpoint instead. We'll document the exact log fields in the privacy section of the installer."
[06:21] Dana Ortiz: "Okay, can we go into the install/values stuff quickly? I opened the sample helm values in the doc."
[06:28] Priya Nair (screen share): "Yep — so in values.yaml we expose fields: image.repo, image.tag, kms.provider, kms.keyArn (or keyName for GCP), network.peering.cidr, controlPlane.endpoint, and marketplace.metadata. There's also a runtime.quant.profile which is the quick toggle for int8. Note: some runtime quant profiles require model-conversion artifacts."
[06:51] Dana Ortiz: "In our case the key ARN is managed in AWS KMS and rotation is handled by our security team. If we give you a role with decrypt permissions scoped to that key, is that sufficient?"

[07:08] Mark Liu: "Are there any egress charges we should be aware of when Redwood pulls the OCI images?"
[07:12] Jordan Blake: "If you allow direct pulls from your registry into Redwood's nodes, standard egress applies per cloud provider. If you mirror to Redwood's marketplace registry, we absorb it on our side for installs. We'll put the cost model in the billing manifest."
[07:24] Dana Ortiz: "One of our legal requirements is an audit for data access in the first 90 days. Does Redwood provide a ready-made evidence package?"
[07:31] Priya Nair: "We provide an audit export: IAM actions, KMS decrypt usage, and control plane access logs, with filters for time ranges. We can produce a SOC-like evidence pack as an add-on."
