title: Lyra MedTech - Reserved GPU & Fallback Qualification
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date: 2026-06-18
- Start: 16:00 UTC
- Duration: ~56 minutes
- Attendees: Jordan Blake (Redwood AE), Sana Iqbal (Redwood SE), Carlos Mendes (CTO, Lyra), Priya Kapoor (Head of MLOps), Aaron Lee (SRE), Mei Chen (Product)

Auto summary (auto, may be rough):
Lyra is evaluating Dedicated reserved capacity for inference of clinical NLP models and multimodal triage models. Primary concerns: tenant isolation for PHI, burst from nightly batches + daytime spikey API calls, predictable latency at 95th percentile, KMS integration and audit logs, fallbacks to hosted models if reserved pool saturated. Discussed warm-pools/warmpre-warm, preemption/eviction tolerances, model quantization to reduce instance footprint, and potential on-prem private control plane in future. Next: pricing + SOC2/KMS docs + burst test.

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan (Redwood): Hey everyone, thanks for making time. I think we said 45, 50 minutes? We've got about an hour on the calendar, so we'll try to be efficient. I'm Jordan, AE at Redwood, and with me is Sana, our solutions engineer.
[00:12] Sana (Redwood): Hi yep hi all. Quick agenda: understand Lyra's workload patterns, isolation and compliance constraints, sizing for reserved GPUs, and then talk burst/fallback strategies.
[00:20] Carlos (Lyra): Thanks. To set context, Lyra runs clinical notes NLP and a triage model that blends text + small images. We're hitting volume — our prod tail gets spiky — lot of API calls late morning with smaller batches at night. PHI is in flight sometimes so isolation and KMS are critical.
[00:36] Priya (Lyra): Our success metrics: P95 latency under 350ms for text-only endpoints, overall cost target is X but we'll share numbers after. We want to avoid noisy neighbor issues and have clear audit logs, plus SSO/SAML.
[00:49] Aaron (Lyra): And from ops perspective, we need predictable eviction behavior, how warm-pools work, and whether we can reserve nodes in a VPC or on-prem later.
[00:58] Jordan: Perfect. Sana?
[01:00] Sana: Sure. Quick high-level: Redwood Dedicated gives you a reserved GPU pool with configurable isolation — single-tenant pools or multi-tenant with strict fencing. We support VPC private peering and KMS integrations; audit logs surface per-request metadata. For burst: we offer warm pools and pre-warmed instances and automatic fallback to compatible hosted variants if local capacity is exhausted.
[01:20] Carlos: When you say "compatible hosted variant" do you mean a smaller model or a cloud-hosted instance that still meets our compliance? We can't have PHI leave our VPC.
[01:30] Sana: Good point. Fallbacks depend on the deployment mode. For Dedicated within your VPC or Private on-prem, fallbacks can be to another reserved pool or an on-prem standby. For Hosted public fallback we wouldn't route PHI there. We can set policy to only fallback to compliant targets.
[01:45] Priya: Are warm-pools charged when idle? How long to spin up a new instance from cold?
[01:52] Sana: Warm-pools are part of the Dedicated reservation — you can configure a percentage as hot standby. They are charged as part of the reserved commitment. Cold boot from scratch is variable; with GPUs it's minutes, with warmpool we aim for sub-30s for new replica attach. For large LLMs with big KV caches, the prefix resume can add time; we mitigate with KV cache snapshot restore and pre-warming.
[02:11] Aaron: We run a mixture of smaller LLMs (7–13B) and one heavier triage model ~70B quantized. Model placement matters — do you support mixed instance types in the same pool (A100 + H100)?
[02:22] Sana: You can mix model variants but not mix hardware in one homogeneous pool for single-tenant strict isolation. You can have multiple pools per account with pinned hardware types and routing policies. For the 70B quantized model we recommend H100 or A100 with 80GB depending on perf targets. Quantization and kernel choices matter.
[02:40] Carlos: What's the approach to KV cache and continuous batching — we've seen other vendors drop tokens due to suboptimal batching.
[02:48] Sana: Redwood runtime uses continuous batching that respects latency SLOs. KV cache is preserved across warm replicas where possible; there are heuristics: if sessions are sticky we keep KV on the same replica; otherwise we use prefix/KV cache snapshot efficient restore. It isn't perfect — sometimes you see slightly higher latency on first warm request.
[03:07] Carlos: Understood. On compliance: KMS — do you support customer-managed keys (CMK) and bring-your-own-key patterns?
[03:14] Sana: Yes, we integrate with KMS providers (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) and HSM via our Private deployment. For Dedicated in customer VPC we can use their CMK for at-rest model artifacts and encryption keys. Audit logs include key usage events.
[03:28] Mei (Lyra): For our timeline — we want pilot in 6 weeks, potential full reserve in 3 months. Is that realistic?
[03:34] Jordan: Absolutely plausible. Pilot -> burst profiling -> contract -> provision. Sana, from an ops lead-time perspective?
[03:40] Sana: Typically pilot provisioning for a small reserved pool + warmpool takes 1–2 weeks once contract is signed and networking is in place. If on-prem Private we allow more lead time for packaging.
[03:48] Aaron: On eviction tolerance: suppose preemption happens or host fails. What's your SLA and what visible signals will we get to orchestrate re-tries?
[03:57] Sana: Our Dedicated SLA covers uptime for reserved pool, but node-level preemption depends on cloud provider. We present graceful drain events and syslog-level hooks; you'll get events via our webhook/trace system. We recommend idempotent retries and configuring request-level timeouts. Also can configure circuit-breaker policies to spill to canary fallback.
[04:16] Carlos: Can we run internal canaries or synthetic traffic to validate burst? We want to simulate a daytime spike and a nightly batch concurrency.
[04:23] Sana: Yes, we'll provide a test harness. We'll also ask you to send representative payloads; we'll run a 1-hour stress profile that simulates your daytime spike and the nightly batches. That will give us a P95 and tail cost estimate.
[04:34] Priya: About networking — we have a strict VPC peering rule. Do you require public endpoints?
[04:39] Jordan: For Dedicated we can do private VPC peering and Private deployment gives full on-prem. We won't require public egress for model inferences if you choose private networking.
[04:48] Aaron: How do we handle data residency for European users? We're US-based but some data is EU bound.
[04:54] Sana: We support region pinning for Dedicated and Private and can instantiate reserved capacity in specific regions to meet residency. Audit logs are available centrally or regionally.
[05:03] Carlos: Pricing talk, high level — reserved pools are a monthly commit, with discount tiers. How flexible is commitment if our usage grows fast?
[05:10] Jordan: We offer ramping plans: initial smaller commit with a defined scale-up option plus short-term burst credits. For big growth we handle re-negotiation at renewal. We'll send pricing tiers in the follow-up.
[05:19] Mei: For our triage model, we currently run quantized 4-bit. Does Redwood recommend different quantization for latency vs cost tradeoffs?
[05:26] Sana: We recommend mixed approach: quantize to 4-bit for cost but run an FP16 or INT8 variant for latency-critical endpoints. Redwood Optimize can suggest profiles per endpoint.
[05:36] Aaron: Any limits on request size or tokenization patterns that might blow up cost? We see big variance in token length.
[05:42] Sana: Token length affects batching and cache. We recommend truncation/early stop heuristics on client side for cost control and to keep latency predictable. Also prefix caching helps if users send repeated prompts.
[05:52] Carlos: Security review path — we'll need your SOC2 docs, pen-test report, and details on RBAC/SSO.
[05:58] Jordan: We'll share SOC2 + compliance package and the KMS integration guide. We'll also include architecture diagrams for VPC/private deployments.
[06:04] Priya: One more — operator experience: can our SREs get token-level cost dashboards and per-route tracing?
[06:10] Sana: Yes, Redwood Console gives per-token cost breakdown, per-route latency histograms, traces, and per-model dashboards. You can integrate with your observability stack via tracing hooks.
[06:19] Carlos: Ok let's recap quickly and assign owners.
[06:22] Jordan: Recap: we'll send pricing & contract template; we'll share SOC2/KMS docs; Sana will prep burst-profile harness and run a 1-hour burst test once Lyra provides sample payloads and traffic pattern; schedule technical deep dive for onprem option.
[06:35] Priya: Sounds good — Priya will send sample traffic and P95 targets by Monday 2026-06-21. Aaron will validate network constraints.
[06:43] Jordan: Action items noted. Any other blockers?
[06:46] Carlos: Not from us. Excited to see the results.
[06:49] Jordan: Great. We'll follow up with the materials by early next week. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript.

References / artifacts mentioned:
- Dedicated pricing sheet (to be attached)
- SOC2 + KMS integration docs (link to be shared)
- Burst profile test harness (Sana to provide script)

Notes about transcription: speaker overlap and some proper nouns may be mis-transcribed (e.g., KV cache sometimes appears as "K B cache").

[01:52] Sana: Warm-pools are part of the Dedicated reservation — you can configure a percentage as hot standby. They are charged as part of the reserved commitment. Cold boot from scratch is variable; with GPUs it's minutes, with warmpool we aim for sub-30s for new replica attach. For large LLMs with big KV caches, the prefix resume can add time; we mitigate with KV cache snapshot restore and pre-warming.
[02:11] Aaron: We run a mixture of smaller LLMs (7–13B) and one heavier triage model ~70B quantized. Model placement matters — do you support mixed instance types in the same pool (A100 + H100)?
[02:22] Sana: You can mix model variants but not mix hardware in one homogeneous pool for single-tenant strict isolation. You can have multiple pools per account with pinned hardware types and routing policies. For the 70B quantized model we recommend H100 or A100 with 80GB depending on perf targets. Quantization and kernel choices matter.
[02:40] Carlos: What's the approach to KV cache and continuous batching — we've seen other vendors drop tokens due to suboptimal batching.
[02:48] Sana: Redwood runtime uses continuous batching that respects latency SLOs. KV cache is preserved across warm replicas where possible; there are heuristics: if sessions are sticky we keep KV on the same replica; otherwise we use prefix/KV cache snapshot efficient restore. It isn't perfect — sometimes you see slightly higher latency on first warm request.
[03:07] Carlos: Understood. On compliance: KMS — do you support customer-managed keys (CMK) and bring-your-own-key patterns?
[03:14] Sana: Yes, we integrate with KMS providers (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) and HSM via our Private deployment. For Dedicated in customer VPC we can use their CMK for at-rest model artifacts and encryption keys. Audit logs include key usage events.
[03:28] Mei (Lyra): For our timeline — we want pilot in 6 weeks, potential full reserve in 3 months. Is that realistic?
[03:34] Jordan: Absolutely plausible. Pilot -> burst profiling -> contract -> provision. Sana, from an ops lead-time perspective?
[03:40] Sana: Typically pilot provisioning for a small reserved pool + warmpool takes 1–2 weeks once contract is signed and networking is in place. If on-prem Private we allow more lead time for packaging.
[03:48] Aaron: On eviction tolerance: suppose preemption happens or host fails. What's your SLA and what visible signals will we get to orchestrate re-tries?
[03:57] Sana: Our Dedicated SLA covers uptime for reserved pool, but node-level preemption depends on cloud provider. We present graceful drain events and syslog-level hooks; you'll get events via our webhook/trace system. We recommend idempotent retries and configuring request-level timeouts. Also can configure circuit-breaker policies to spill to canary fallback.
[04:16] Carlos: Can we run internal canaries or synthetic traffic to validate burst? We want to simulate a daytime spike and a nightly batch concurrency.
[04:23] Sana: Yes, we'll provide a test harness. We'll also ask you to send representative payloads; we'll run a 1-hour stress profile that simulates your daytime spike and the nightly batches. That will give us a P95 and tail cost estimate.
[04:34] Priya: About networking — we have a strict VPC peering rule. Do you require public endpoints?
[04:39] Jordan: For Dedicated we can do private VPC peering and Private deployment gives full on-prem. We won't require public egress for model inferences if you choose private networking.
[04:48] Aaron: How do we handle data residency for European users? We're US-based but some data is EU bound.
[04:54] Sana: We support region pinning for Dedicated and Private and can instantiate reserved capacity in specific regions to meet residency. Audit logs are available centrally or regionally.
[05:03] Carlos: Pricing talk, high level — reserved pools are a monthly commit, with discount tiers. How flexible is commitment if our usage grows fast?
[05:10] Jordan: We offer ramping plans: initial smaller commit with a defined scale-up option plus short-term burst credits. For big growth we handle re-negotiation at renewal. We'll send pricing tiers in the follow-up.
[05:19] Mei: For our triage model, we currently run quantized 4-bit. Does Redwood recommend different quantization for latency vs cost tradeoffs?
[05:26] Sana: We recommend mixed approach: quantize to 4-bit for cost but run an FP16 or INT8 variant for latency-critical endpoints. Redwood Optimize can suggest profiles per endpoint.
[05:36] Aaron: Any limits on request size or tokenization patterns that might blow up cost? We see big variance in token length.
[05:42] Sana: Token length affects batching and cache. We recommend truncation/early stop heuristics on client side for cost control and to keep latency predictable. Also prefix caching helps if users send repeated prompts.
[05:52] Carlos: Security review path — we'll need your SOC2 docs, pen-test report, and details on RBAC/SSO.
[05:58] Jordan: We'll share SOC2 + compliance package and the KMS integration guide. We'll also include architecture diagrams for VPC/private deployments.
[06:04] Priya: One more — operator experience: can our SREs get token-level cost dashboards and per-route tracing?
[06:10] Sana: Yes, Redwood Console gives per-token cost breakdown, per-route latency histograms, traces, and per-model dashboards. You can integrate with your observability stack via tracing hooks.
[06:19] Carlos: Ok let's recap quickly and assign owners.
[06:22] Jordan: Recap: we'll send pricing & contract template; we'll share SOC2/KMS docs; Sana will prep burst-profile harness and run a 1-hour burst test once Lyra provides sample payloads and traffic pattern; schedule technical deep dive for onprem option.
[06:35] Priya: Sounds good — Priya will send sample traffic and P95 targets by Monday 2026-06-21. Aaron will validate network constraints.
[06:43] Jordan: Action items noted. Any other blockers?
[06:46] Carlos: Not from us. Excited to see the results.
[06:49] Jordan: Great. We'll follow up with the materials by early next week. Thanks everyone.

End of transcript.

title: GPU inference tuning: cudagraph fallback, kernel paths & kv-cache behavior
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-06-16
Start: 09:32 UTC
Duration: 58 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Enzo (Redwood Perf), Mark (Helios), Sophia (Helios), Rita (Helios Security)

Summary:
Short deep dive focused on GPU runtime behavior when CUDA graphs are enabled, fallback behavior when graphs are unstable, kernel-path selection between TensorRT-LLM and vLLM backends, and the interaction with persistent KV cache and batching. Customer shared production traces and constraints: 8k monthly users, strict 95th percentile latency SLO of 220ms for short chat flows, and on-prem VPC deployment preference for IP-restricted environments.

Topics discussed:
- CUDA graph stability, warm vs cold runs
- Kernel selection heuristics: fused attention ops, rotary/rope handling, flash attention variants
- TRT-LLM vs vLLM: memory layout, workspace, and prepacking costs
- Persistent KV cache eviction and prefetching behavior under batch mixing
- Quantization impacts on kernel choice and fallback to reference kernels
- VPC/on-prem constraints: driver versions, NUMA, and allowed kernel modules

Transcript:
[00:00:00] Jordan: alright — hey everyone thanks for squeezing this in, um so quick context, Priya and Enzo from infra/perf will drive the deep dive, we'll try to focus on the CUDA graph failure modes you saw.
[00:00:12] Mark: yeah thanks, appreciate it. we saw a few tail spikes over the weekend where the 95th jumped to ~480ms, and our logs show some cudagraph instantiation errors followed by long path fallbacks.
[00:00:25] Priya: ok that's helpful, do you have the trace? I saw the snippet you uploaded but want to confirm: are these mostly short chat requests with prompts under 256 tokens?
[00:00:33] Mark: mostly sub-150 token replies, yes. We also intermix autocomplete streams for code and chat.
[00:00:40] Enzo: quick clarifier — when you say cudagraph instantiation error, is it an assertion at graph capture, OOM, or mismatch at replay time? sometimes driver versions show different symptoms.
[00:00:51] Sophia: the error strings were a mix, sometimes cudagraph::capture_failed, sometimes an internal assert in TRT-LLM path. we forwarded logs — might be version skid between our driver and the server image.
[00:01:04] Priya: okay — two things cause us trouble in practice: dynamic shapes / variable-length KV cache growth during capture, and kernels that switch execpaths due to quantization or attention algorithm toggles. capture expects deterministic exec path.
[00:01:20] Mark: yeah that jives. we also saw that when we enabled persistent KV cache, the first few warmups were fine then later we had evictions and then the graphs flapped.
[00:01:31] Jordan: can you describe evictions — are they eviction due to LRU hitting memory budget or NUMA migrations?
[00:01:38] Sophia: mostly memory pressure under mixed batch sizes, on our dual-socket test machines we do see NUMA bouncing when threads get scheduled across sockets.
[00:01:47] Enzo: that is a big hint. CUDA graphs capture buffers/addresses; if kernel chooses a different workspace layout after an eviction or if an allocator returns a different pointer, replay can break. TRT-LLM tends to prepack and allocate workspace at init which is more stable but has higher upfront cost; vLLM's continuous-batching paths can see more pointer churn.
[00:02:05] Mark: interesting — so is the recommendation to prefer TRT-LLM for predictable latency?
[00:02:11] Priya: it's nuanced. TRT-LLM gives tight 95th for steady-state with similar shapes — but it can struggle with heterogeneous workloads, long-tail prompt lengths, and some quantized kernels may not have TRT equivalents, forcing us to fallback. vLLM gives more flexible exec paths and often recovers better after eviction because it relies on standard CUDA kernels.
[00:02:29] Rita: from security side we need deterministic behavior and reproducible performance — graph flapping is a regression for ops budgets. also curious about SSO/KMS impacts if we move on-prem.
[00:02:39] Jordan: we'll cover compliance items in the follow-up doc; for now surface-level: Redwood private supports KMS and HSM integrations, SOC2 artifacts are available. we'll attach the doc after the call.
[00:02:49] [00:03:00] Priya: back to graphs — we ran matrix tests with three axes: graph enabled vs disabled, persistent KV cache on vs off, and batch-mixing (single-size batches vs mixed). results: with graph+kvcache persistent we saw best median but worst tail when eviction events occurred. disabling graphs reduced tail but increased median by ~12–18ms.
[00:03:25] Enzo: add to that quantization effects: 4-bit quant in our runtime uses specialized fused kernels (rotary+flash), but those fused kernels often don't have safe cudagraph-friendly captures across dependent allocations. the graph capture will succeed for small sequences but fail once sequence length or key/value set grows.
[00:03:44] Mark: is there an automatic fallback? in our logs we saw 'fallback to dynamic path' but the latency hit was large.
[00:03:51] Priya: yes we have a fallback — the runtime will try to replay the graph; on failure it falls back to non-graph path and marks the input shape for future runs. unfortunately fallback has extra compilation/prep cost and triggers extra GC in some runtimes, hence the spike.
[00:04:06] Sophia: could the runtime proactively detect churn and avoid capture if it will likely fail?
[00:04:11] Enzo: that's what our recent heuristics do — we maintain a capture stability counter per route. if eviction rate or pointer churn exceeds threshold, we skip capture for that route. however thresholds must be tuned per workload; Helios's mixed short-chat + code completions is borderline.
[00:04:27] Mark: what about prefetching KV cache so eviction doesn't happen? we can increase host memory but want guidance on costs.
[00:04:34] Priya: prefetching reduces eviction, but increases steady-state memory—tradeoff between cost and latency SLO. we can recommend a sliding window prefetch: keep the most recent N steps pinned on GPU and evict older. we can simulate what N looks like given your trace.
[00:04:49] Jordan: action item — we'll run sims using the trace you provided and show memory/latency curves for different pinned window sizes.
[00:04:57] [00:05:00] Rita: NUMA/migration — does Redwood have guidance on thread pinning and driver versions for on-prem? our ops team prefers image-level installs.
[00:05:09] Priya: yes — recommend CPU isolation for the GPU server: pin inference threads to the local socket, disable power management features that migrate threads, and we prefer CUDA 12.x+ with specific NVIDIA driver matching the TRT-LLM build. we'll include the exact matrix in the doc.
[00:05:23] Enzo: also we saw cases where kernel selection toggled between flash attention and fallback with subtle sequence length changes. that sometimes came from the allocator returning different alignment and triggering a different code path. it's rare but exists.
[00:05:36] Mark: so how do we choose between TRT-LLM and vLLM on our Dedicated pool? we want predictable 95th but also want cost controls.
[00:05:45] Priya: recommendation: for stable routes (very regular prompt shapes), prefer TRT-LLM with graphs. if you have heavy tail or many shapes, prefer vLLM dynamic paths or hybrid: use TRT for hot route and vLLM fallback for diverse routes. Redwood's smart routing can send requests by policy.
[00:06:01] Jordan: example: route chat-short -> TRT with persistent kv pinned window of 64 tokens; route code-complete -> vLLM dynamic with batching tuned for throughput.
[00:06:10] [00:06:20] Mark: about quantization — if we quant to 4-bit, are we losing TRT kernel support?
[00:06:25] Enzo: some TRT kernels support quantized paths but vendor coverage varies. in many models we use custom fused kernels for 4-bit that are not identical between backends — that leads to different memory footprints and sometimes incompatibilities with cudagraph capture. So quantization introduces both performance gains and compatibility risks.
[00:06:41] Priya: we advise running a correctness and perf sweep: run sample prompts through both quant and fp16; check for regressions. Redwood Optimize can auto-suggest quant profiles based on your trace and evaluation prompts.
[00:06:53] Sophia: that would help — tell us more about the profiling harness you used to produce prepack and capture timings.
[00:06:59] Enzo: we have a microbench harness that replays your trace on a single host, toggles graph capture, and measures: graph-capture time, first-replay latency, steady-state latency, and fallback penalty. it also records allocator pointer stability stats and kernel choices per step. we'll run that on your trace.
[00:07:19] Mark: any immediate knobs we can set in our infra to reduce tail without big infra changes?
[00:07:23] Priya: short list: 1) disable cudagraphs for mixed-size routes, 2) increase pinned KV window slightly, 3) configure thread pinning to avoid NUMA cross-sockets, 4) upgrade driver to recommended matrix, 5) set conservative capture thresholds. These reduce tail with small ops changes.
[00:07:41] Rita: on compliance, the one worry is when we run on-prem we need KMS + audit logs. is the audit trail preserved when runtime falls back?
[00:07:49] Jordan: yes, logs/traces are preserved and we can stream them to your SIEM; we also keep per-request routing and kernel-path metadata for auditing. We'll include log schema in the security doc.
[00:07:58] [00:08:10] Priya: a few gotchas we noted in our experiments: - CUDA graph + unified memory can be unstable if OS page-faults happen during replay; avoid UVM for low-latency routes. - pinned memory alignment matters for fused kernels. - quantized fused kernels might bypass some workspace checks and cause silent correctness diffs: must run eval sets.
[00:08:33] Enzo: one more: when TRT-LLM prepacks weights, the startup cost increases; on bursty workloads that spin up containers per-request this cost is painful — use warm pools or keep instances hot for hot routes.
[00:08:46] Mark: we are looking at Dedicated plus Private for certain customers. how much ops overhead is there to keep warm pools?
[00:08:52] Priya: modest — reserved instances with autoscaler and warm pool heuristics in Redwood Dedicated manage that; cost versus latency tradeoff is predictable and we can show simulation.
[00:09:03] [00:09:10] Jordan: recap and next steps: we'll run the cudagraph fallback matrix with your trace, simulate pinned KV windows and memory curves, and produce recommended config per route. Priya will draft kernel selection heuristics and quantization guidance. we'll attach SOC2 + KMS docs and the driver/kernel matrix.
[00:09:28] Mark: thanks, that sounds good. can we get the results by next week?
[00:09:32] Jordan: yes, target 2026-06-23.
[00:09:37] Sophia: and we'll share an expanded trace including some long prompts and mixed clients.
[00:09:42] Rita: I'll send the security checklist end of day tomorrow.
[00:09:47] Jordan: great — thanks everyone. we'll follow up over email with attachments and schedule a short check-in once we have results.

Questions captured:
- What driver/CUDA/NUMA combinations are supported for stable cudagraph capture?
- How does persistent KV cache eviction interact with CUDA graphs and pointer stability?
- What quant profiles keep TRT kernel compatibility?

References / Links mentioned:
- Helios trace upload (link in CRM)
- Redwood SOC2 + KMS integration doc (to be attached)
- Example benchmark spreadsheet (Redwood perf sheet link)

End of transcript.
